> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECST832 - WEB PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Styling Web Page Using CSS: Introduction, Inline Styles, Embedded Style Sheets, Linking External Style Sheets, Positioning Elements, Absolute Positioning, z-index, Relative Positioning, Span, Backgrounds, Element Dimensions, Box Model and Text Flow, Media Types and Media Queries, Drop-Down Menus ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is CSS? **Cascading Style Sheets (CSS)** is a stylesheet language used to describe the presentation of a document written in HTML or XML. According to the W3C specification, CSS separates **content (HTML)** from **presentation (CSS)** — enabling developers to control layout, colors, fonts, spacing, and even animations across multiple web pages from a single file. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** CSS is a *rule-based* language that applies styling rules to HTML elements using *selectors* and *declarations*. The "cascading" in CSS refers to the priority scheme (specificity, importance, source order) used to determine which rule wins when multiple rules target the same element. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The House and the Paint" Think of an HTML page as the **structural skeleton of a house** — walls, doors, and rooms (the `
`, `

`, `

` elements). CSS is the **interior designer and painter** who decides: - What **color** are the walls? (background-color) - What **size** are the windows? (width, height) - Where should the **sofa** sit? (positioning) - How should the rooms **flow** from one to another? (margin, padding, text-flow) Without CSS, every webpage would look like a plain 1990s academic document — black text on white background, with no layout control. With CSS, we get the modern, responsive, beautifully-designed web. ### 1.3 Anatomy of a CSS Rule A CSS rule consists of two main parts: 1. **Selector** — the HTML element(s) to style 2. **Declaration Block** — one or more declarations enclosed in `{ }` Each declaration contains a **property** and a **value**, separated by a colon `:` and terminated by a semicolon `;`. ```css h1 { color: navy; font-size: 32px; text-align: center; } ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A *property* is the visual attribute being changed (e.g., `color`). A *value* is the setting for that attribute (e.g., `navy`, `#000080`, `rgb(0,0,128)`). ### 1.4 The Three Ways to Apply CSS | Method | Where Applied | Best Used For | KTU Frequency | |---|---|---|---| | **Inline Styles** | Inside the HTML `style` attribute | Quick testing, one-off overrides | Medium | | **Embedded (Internal) Style Sheet** | Inside `

KTU Web Programming

``` > [!NOTE] > Embedded styles are useful for single-page applications or when external CSS would be overkill. They have higher priority than external styles if both target the same selector equally. ### 2.3 Linking External Style Sheets External CSS is the **industry standard**. A `.css` file is created separately and linked via the `` element in the HTML ``. **Step 1 — Create `styles.css`:** ```css /* styles.css */ body { background-color: #f4f4f4; font-family: 'Segoe UI', sans-serif; } .container { width: 80%; margin: 0 auto; } ``` **Step 2 — Link from HTML:** ```html ``` **The `rel`, `type`, and `href` attributes:** - `rel="stylesheet"` — relationship: this is a stylesheet - `type="text/css"` — MIME type (optional in HTML5) - `href` — path to the CSS file > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Favorite Question:** *"Differentiate between Embedded and External style sheets."* Memorize: Embedded = `

KTU Web Programming — Media Query Demo

Resize the browser window to see the background change.

``` **External file `screen.css`:** ```css h1 { color: #2c3e50; } p { font-size: 16px; line-height: 1.6; } ``` **Explanation of breakpoints:** $$ \text{Background}(w) = \begin{cases} \text{lightblue} & \text{if } w < 600 \\ \text{white} & \text{if } 600 \leq w < 1024 \\ \text{lightgreen} & \text{if } w \geq 1024 \end{cases} $$ The `viewport` meta tag ensures proper scaling on mobile devices — a KTU-expected best practice. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the `viewport` meta tag** in responsive design questions — costs 1–2 marks. > 2. **Using inline styles in production** — examiners deduct marks for bad practice. > 3. **Confusing `position: relative` with `position: absolute`** — relative preserves space; absolute removes it. > 4. **Forgetting `box-sizing: border-box`** in box model calculations — the formula changes drastically. > 5. **Not mentioning the cascade order** (specificity → source order) in cascade questions. > 6. **Writing `display: none` in drop-down menus** but forgetting the `:hover` trigger on the parent. > 7. **Using `z-index` on `position: static` elements** — it has no effect. > 8. **Mixing up `vw`/`vh` with `%`** — `vw`/`vh` is viewport-relative; `%` is parent-relative. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **CSS** separates presentation from structure; three ways to apply it: inline, embedded, external. - **Inline styles** use the `style` attribute and have the highest specificity (1,0,0,0). - **Embedded styles** use `

topics.title: Extensible Markup Language: Introduction, XML Basics, Structuring Data, XML Namespaces, Document Type Definitions (DTDs), XML Vocabularies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECST832 - WEB PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Extensible Markup Language: Introduction, XML Basics, Structuring Data, XML Namespaces, Document Type Definitions (DTDs), XML Vocabularies # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is XML? (The Formal KTU Definition) **Extensible Markup Language (XML)** is a W3C-recommended, text-based, platform-independent markup language designed to store, transport, and structure data in a self-describing, human-readable, and machine-parseable format. Unlike HTML (which is *presentational*), XML is purely *descriptive* — it carries information about what the data *is*, not how it should *look*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition to Memorize:** > "XML is a meta-language that allows the design of customized markup languages to encode documents and structured data in a format that is both human-readable and machine-readable." ### Key W3C Properties of XML (KTU 2024 Highlight) - **Extensibility** — Users can define their own tags. - **Self-describing** — Tags describe the nature of the content. - **Platform & Vendor Independent** — Pure ASCII/Unicode text. - **Strictly Structured** — A document is either *well-formed* or *valid*. - **Separation of Content from Presentation** — Data and display are decoupled. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Universal Shipping Container" Imagine you are a manufacturer in Kerala shipping goods to customers across the world. If every customer demanded a different box dimension, shipping would be chaos. So you create a **Universal Shipping Container** — a standardized box with: - **A standard outer shape** that any ship, train, or truck can carry. - **Labels on the outside** that say exactly what is inside (e.g., "FRAGILE", "ELECTRONICS", "10 KG"). - **Custom interior compartments** that the manufacturer designs based on the product. | Real-World Concept | XML Equivalent | |---|---| | Universal Container Shape | XML Syntax Rules (well-formedness) | | Standard Outside Labels | XML Tags (e.g., ``, ``) | | Customs/Quality Inspection | **DTD / XML Schema** (Validation) | | Manufacturer's Custom Layout | **User-defined Elements/Attributes** | | Container Number & Origin Code | **XML Namespaces** (avoiding tag conflicts) | | Ship → Train → Truck (any medium) | **Platform Independence** (HTTP, SMTP, Files) | > [!NOTE] > **Just like a Universal Container revolutionized global trade, XML revolutionized global data exchange** by providing one standard format that any system on earth can read, write, and validate. --- ## 1.3 The Origin Story — Quick Historical Context XML was developed by the **XML Working Group** under the **World Wide Web Consortium (W3C)** in 1996–1998 and became a W3C Recommendation on **10 February 1998**. It was designed as a simplified subset of **SGML (Standard Generalized Markup Language)** — powerful, but too complex for web use. The goal was to bring SGML's data-structuring power to the web with HTML-like simplicity. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic to remember the lineage:** `SGML → HTML + XML` (HTML is for display, XML is for data). --- ## 1.4 XML vs HTML — The Crucial Distinction | Feature | HTML | XML | |---|---|---| | Purpose | Display data | Describe & Transport data | | Tag Set | Predefined, fixed | User-defined, extensible | | Case Sensitivity | Insensitive | **Sensitive** (`` ≠ ``) | | Closing Tags | Optional for some (e.g., `
`) | **Mandatory** for all | | Errors | Browser forgives | Parser **stops on first error** | | Endorsed by | W3C | **W3C** | --- ## 1.5 Real-World Use Cases of XML - **Web Services:** SOAP, WSDL, REST-XML payloads - **Configuration Files:** `web.xml`, `pom.xml`, `AndroidManifest.xml`, `server.xml` - **Data Exchange:** Banking (FIXML), Healthcare (HL7), Publishing (DocBook) - **Document Formats:** Office Open XML (`.docx`, `.xlsx`), ODF, RSS, Atom - **Mobile & Android:** Layout resources, build manifests # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of an XML Document An XML document has **two logical layers** — the **Physical Layer (Storage)** and the **Logical Layer (Information Set / Infoset)**. Every well-formed XML document contains the following components in order: ### Logical Structure (Top-to-Bottom) 1. **XML Declaration (Prolog)** — Optional, but recommended. Must be on line 1. 2. **Optional Processing Instructions & Comments** 3. **Document Type Declaration** — Links to DTD (optional) 4. **Root Element** — Exactly **one** per document; the *document element*. 5. **Child Elements, Attributes, Text, Entities** — All nested inside the root. ### The XML Declaration ```xml ``` | Attribute | Purpose | Allowed Values | |---|---|---| | `version` | XML version | `1.0` (or `1.1`) | | `encoding` | Character set | `UTF-8`, `UTF-16`, `ISO-8859-1` | | `standalone` | External DTD reference? | `yes` / `no` | --- ## 2.2 Core Building Blocks of XML ### A. Elements The primary container for data. Formed by a *start tag*, *content*, and an *end tag*. ```xml Web Programming ``` > [!NOTE] > **Empty Elements** may use shorthand self-closing syntax: `` ### B. Attributes Provide **metadata** *about* an element. Always appear in the start tag as `name="value"`. ```xml ... ``` ### C. Entity References A mechanism to represent reserved or special characters. | Entity | Represents | Numeric Ref | |---|---|---| | `<` | `<` (less than) | `<` | | `>` | `>` (greater than) | `>` | | `&` | `&` (ampersand) | `&` | | `'` | `'` (apostrophe) | `'` | | `"` | `"` (quote) | `"` | ### D. CDATA Sections Used to embed blocks of text containing many special characters **without escaping**. ```xml d ]]> ``` ### E. Comments ```xml ``` ### F. Processing Instructions (PIs) Pass instructions to a specific application, e.g., a stylesheet link: ```xml ``` --- ## 2.3 Well-Formed vs Valid XML (KTU High-Yield Distinction) > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is a guaranteed 3-mark question in KTU exams.** | Criterion | Well-Formed XML | Valid XML | |---|---|---| | Follows XML syntax rules? | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | | Conforms to a DTD/Schema? | ❌ No requirement | ✅ **Mandatory** | | Who checks it? | **XML Parser** | **Validating Parser** | | Syntactic vs Semantic | Syntactic | Semantic + Syntactic | ### The 10 Rules of XML Well-Formedness (Cheat Sheet) 1. There must be **exactly one root element**. 2. Every start tag must have a matching end tag. 3. Tags are **case-sensitive**. 4. Tags must be **properly nested** (no overlap). 5. Attribute values **must be quoted**. 6. An element cannot have **two attributes with the same name**. 7. Comments cannot contain `--`. 8. Reserved characters must use **entity references** in text. 9. The `&` character cannot start a tag/entity casually. 10. The prolog (if present) must be the very first thing with **no whitespace before**. --- ## 2.4 Document Type Definition (DTD) — Formal Contract A **DTD** is a set of *declarative rules* that defines the legal building blocks of an XML document. It specifies: - The **element names** allowed - The **content model** (sequence, choice, repetition) for each element - The **attributes** allowed, with their types and defaults - The **entities** that can be used ### Two Flavors of DTDs | Type | Location | Keyword | |---|---|---| | **Internal DTD Subset** | Inside the XML document in `` | `[ ... ]` | | **External DTD Subset** | In a separate `.dtd` file | `SYSTEM` or `PUBLIC` | | **Combined** | Both internal + external | Merged by parser | ### Example — Internal DTD ```xml ]> Web Programming Ramesh 499 ``` ### DTD Element Declaration Operators (KTU Must-Know) | Operator | Meaning | Example | |---|---|---| | (No operator) | **Sequence** — must appear in order | `(title, author)` | | `vert` | **Choice** — one of | `(hardcover vert paperback)` | | `?` | Optional (0 or 1) | `(edition?)` | | `*` | Zero or more | `(review*)` | | `+` | One or more | `(chapter+)` | | `,` | Sequence delimiter | `(a, b, c)` | ### DTD Attribute Types | Type | Meaning | Example | |---|---|---| | `CDATA` | Character data | `` | | `ID` | Unique identifier in doc | `id ID #REQUIRED` | | `IDREF` | Refers to another ID | `ref IDREF #IMPLIED` | | `(a vert b vert c)` | Enumerated | `type (fiction vert non-fiction) "fiction"` | | `NMTOKEN` | One valid XML name token | — | ### DTD Attribute Default Values | Keyword | Behavior | |---|---| | `#REQUIRED` | Attribute **must** be present | | `#IMPLIED` | Attribute is **optional** | | `#FIXED "x"` | Must be the literal `"x"` | | `"default"` | Default if not provided | --- ## 2.5 XML Namespaces — Solving the Tag Collision Problem When documents are merged from multiple XML vocabularies, the **same tag name can mean different things**. Example: `` could mean a *furniture table* or an *HTML table*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Namespaces give tags a globally unique, qualified identity** to prevent ambiguity. ### Declaring a Namespace ```xml HTML Coffee Table ``` ### Namespace Rules - **Default namespace:** `xmlns="http://www.w3.org/..."` (no prefix → applies to unqualified elements) - **Namespace URI** is just a **unique string identifier**; the parser does **not** fetch it. - **Prefixes** are bound to URIs via `xmlns:prefix="URI"`. - Once declared, a prefix can be used throughout that element's scope. - A prefix declared on the **root** is inherited by all descendants. ### Why URIs and Not URLs? URIs are used as **unique opaque strings**, not as addresses. The W3C uses URIs because they are guaranteed to be globally unique when managed as namespaces. A URL works for the same reason. --- ## 2.6 XML Vocabularies (Domain-Specific XML Dialects) An **XML Vocabulary** is a collection of XML element and attribute names designed for a *specific domain*. They are essentially "XML-based languages" that live on top of XML syntax. | Vocabulary | Domain | KTU Mention | |---|---|---| | **MathML** | Mathematical notation | W3C standard | | **SVG** | Vector graphics | W3C standard | | **XHTML** | Web pages (XML-compliant HTML) | W3C standard | | **DocBook** | Technical documentation | Publishing | | **RSS / Atom** | News feeds | Web syndication | | **SOAP** | Web service messaging | WSDL partner | | **WSDL** | Web service description | W3C standard | | **XSL-FO** | Formatting objects | Publishing | | **UBL** | Universal Business Language | e-Invoicing | | **OOXML / ODF** | Office documents | Microsoft / OASIS | > [!NOTE] > **Analogy:** If XML is "the alphabet", then a vocabulary is "a language" like English or Hindi — built using the same underlying characters but with its own grammar rules and dictionary. --- ## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet Table | Concept | One-Line Definition | Example | |---|---|---| | **Element** | Tagged data block | `499` | | **Attribute** | Property of an element | `id="101"` | | **Entity** | Symbolic name for data | `©` | | **CDATA** | Raw text section | `` | | **PI** | App-specific instruction | `` | | **DTD** | Structural grammar | `` | | **Namespace** | Tag-collision resolver | `xmlns:x="..."` | | **Vocabulary** | Domain-specific XML dialect | MathML, SVG | | **Well-formed** | Syntactically correct | Parser-accepted | | **Valid** | DTD-conforming | Validator-accepted | # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Building a Valid XML Document from a Specification **Problem Statement (typical KTU exam style):** > *"Design a valid XML document for a university to store information about students. Each student must have a roll number (unique), name, and must be enrolled in one or more courses. Each course has a course code, title, and credits. The document must be validated against a DTD and use a namespace prefix `u:` bound to `http://university.edu/students`."* ### Step 1 — Identify the Information Model First, we extract the entities and their relationships: | Entity | Attributes | Cardinality | |---|---|---| | University | Contains many Students | 1 | | Student | rollNo, name | 1..n (inside University) | | Course | code, title, credits | 1..n (inside Student) | | Enrollment | Links Student to Course | n..m | ### Step 2 — Translate Cardinality into DTD Operators Using the DTD operators from Section 2.4: $$ \text{University} \rightarrow \text{Student}^+ \quad (\text{1 or more students}) $$ $$ \text{Student} \rightarrow \text{rollNo}, \text{name}, \text{Course}^+ \quad (\text{name + 1+ courses}) $$ $$ \text{Course} \rightarrow \text{code}, \text{title}, \text{credits} $$ ### Step 3 — Construct the Internal DTD ```xml ]> ``` ### Step 4 — Construct the XML Instance Document ```xml ]> Anand Krishnan Web Programming 4 Meera Nair Data Structures 3 ``` ### Step 5 — Validate the Document Against DTD | Check | Result | Reason | |---|---|---| | Well-formed XML syntax? | ✅ Pass | Proper nesting, quoted attributes, single root | | `` contains `student+`? | ✅ Pass | 2 `` elements present | | Each `` has `name` then `course+`? | ✅ Pass | Order preserved, ≥1 course each | | `rollNo` is unique `ID`? | ✅ Pass | `S001` ≠ `S002` | | `branch` defaulted? | ✅ Pass | Student 1 has `branch="CSE"` | | `level` matches `(UG vert PG)`? | ✅ Pass | Enumeration satisfied | | `grade` declared in internal subset? | ✅ Pass | Merged with external DTD | ### Step 6 — Namespace Resolution Trace When the parser encounters ``: $$ \text{prefix} = u \quad\longrightarrow\quad \text{Look up } xmlns:u = \text{"http://university.edu/students"} $$ $$ \text{Resolved QName} = \left\{ \text{URI}, \text{localName} \right\} = \left\{ \text{http://university.edu/students}, \text{student} \right\} $$ The parser then matches the **expanded name** to the DTD element declaration. If the DTD declares ``, the validation can be performed because the namespace mapping is applied *after* DTD lookup by default in DTD-based validation. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: XML Parsing Decision Tree When an XML parser reads a document, it follows this logic: ``` START → Read Prolog → Read DOCTYPE ↓ Locate Root Element ↓ For each token: ├─ Start tag? → Push to stack, validate against DTD ├─ End tag? → Pop stack, verify match ├─ Text? → Validate as PCDATA (no raw <, &) ├─ Comment? → Ignore (verify no --) ├─ PI? → Forward to target app └─ CDATA? → Read raw until ]]> ↓ Stack empty at root close? ├─ Yes → WELL-FORMED ✅ └─ No → ERROR (mismatched tags) ↓ If DTD present: ├─ All elements declared? → Check ├─ All content models satisfied? → Check ├─ All attribute rules met? → Check └─ All IDREF references resolve? → Check ↓ VALID ✅ ``` --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU Pitfalls | Pitfall | Why It Fails | KTU Mark Loss | |---|---|---| | `` vs `` used together | Case-sensitive mismatch | 1–2 marks | | `

` | Improper overlap (forbidden) | 2 marks | | `5 < 10` | Raw `<` in text | 1 mark | | `id="101"` (numeric) | `ID` must start with a letter | 2 marks | | Two roots: `` and `` | XML allows only one root | 2 marks | | Forgetting namespace declaration | Undeclared `prefix:tag` errors | 2–3 marks | | Unquoted attribute value `` | Mandatory quoting | 1 mark | | Self-closing without space `
` | Correct: `
` (recommended) | Stylistic only | --- ## 3.4 Mathematical Model: Cardinality Expansion For a content model with $n$ elements having cardinalities $c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_n$, the **maximum number of child elements** in a single parent is: $$ C_{\max} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} c_{i,\max} $$ where $c_{i,\max}$ is the maximum repetition count for the $i$-th element: $$ c_{i,\max} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if operator is `?`} \\ \infty & \text{if operator is `*` or `+`} \\ 1 & \text{if no operator (sequence/choice)} \end{cases} $$ For our university DTD: $$ C_{\max}(\text{university}) = 1_{\text{univ}} \times \infty_{\text{student+}} = \infty $$ $$ C_{\max}(\text{student}) = 1_{\text{name}} \times \infty_{\text{course+}} = \infty $$ This confirms the model is **unbounded** in the number of students and courses — appropriate for a real university. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 XML Document Tree Architecture ```mermaid graph TD A0[xmlDeclaration version 1.0 encoding UTF 8] --> A1 A1[DOCTYPE bookstore Internal DTD] --> A2 A2[root bookstore] --> A3 A3[book id B101] --> A4 A4[title Web Programming] --> A5 A5[author Ramesh] --> A6 A6[price 499] A2 --> B3 B3[book id B102] --> B4 B4[title XML Basics] --> B5 B5[author Suresh] --> B6 B6[price 350] ``` ## 4.2 DTD Validation Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD N0[Start XML Parser] --> N1[Read XML Document] N1 --> N2{DTD Declared?} N2 -- No --> N3[Check Well Formedness Only] N3 --> N4[Output Parsed Infoset] N2 -- Yes --> N5[Load Internal DTD Subset] N5 --> N6[Load External DTD Subset via SYSTEM] N6 --> N7[Merge DTD Subsets] N7 --> N8[Parse Each Element] N8 --> N9{Element Declared in DTD?} N9 -- No --> N10[ERROR Undeclared Element] N9 -- Yes --> N11{Content Model Satisfied?} N11 -- No --> N12[ERROR Invalid Content] N11 -- Yes --> N13{Attributes Match ATTLIST?} N13 -- No --> N14[ERROR Invalid Attribute] N13 -- Yes --> N15[Accept Token] N15 --> N8 N8 --> N16{End of Document?} N16 -- No --> N8 N16 -- Yes --> N17[Output Valid Document] ``` ## 4.3 Namespace Resolution & Tag Collision Avoidance ```mermaid graph LR P1[Bookstore Document] --> P2[Root Element] P2 --> P3[xmlns h HTML namespace] P2 --> P4[xmlns f Furniture namespace] P3 --> P5[h table meaning HTML Table] P4 --> P6[f table meaning Furniture Table] P3 --> P7[h tr meaning HTML Row] P4 --> P8[f material meaning Furniture Material] style P1 fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b style P5 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style P6 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style P7 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style P8 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 ``` ## 4.4 XML Vocabulary Ecosystem (Domain Layering) ```mermaid graph TD L0[SGML Parent Standard 1986] --> L1[XML W3C 1998] L1 --> L2[MathML] L1 --> L3[SVG] L1 --> L4[XHTML] L1 --> L5[RSS Atom] L1 --> L6[SOAP WSDL] L1 --> L7[OOXML ODF] L1 --> L8[DocBook] L2 --> L2a[Mathematics] L3 --> L3a[Graphics] L4 --> L4a[Web Pages] L5 --> L5a[News Feeds] L6 --> L6a[Web Services] L7 --> L7a[Office Documents] L8 --> L8a[Publishing] style L0 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c style L1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1 style L2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style L3 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style L4 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style L5 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style L6 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style L7 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style L8 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 ``` ## 4.5 Well-Formed vs Valid Decision Block ```mermaid graph TD Q0[XML Document] --> Q1{Syntax Correct?} Q1 -- No --> Q2[Rejected by Parser] Q1 -- Yes --> Q3[Well Formed Document] Q3 --> Q4{DTD or Schema Present?} Q4 -- No --> Q5[Well Formed XML No Validation] Q4 -- Yes --> Q6{Conforms to DTD?} Q6 -- No --> Q7[Well Formed but Invalid] Q6 -- Yes --> Q8[Valid XML Document] style Q0 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0277bd style Q3 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825 style Q5 fill:#dcedc8,stroke:#33691e style Q7 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style Q8 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20 ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Differentiate between Well-Formed and Valid XML documents. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **well-formed XML document** is one that strictly obeys the **syntactic rules** of XML (single root element, properly nested tags, quoted attribute values, case-sensitivity, matching start/end tags, etc.). Any standard XML parser can process it. A **valid XML document** is a well-formed document that **additionally conforms to a Document Type Definition (DTD) or XML Schema**. Validation is an optional, semantic check that ensures the document's element names, attribute names, content order, and occurrence counts match the rules defined in the schema. | Criterion | Well-Formed | Valid | |---|---|---| | Follows XML syntax | ✅ | ✅ | | Conforms to DTD/Schema | ❌ Not required | ✅ **Mandatory** | | Checked by | Parser | Validating parser | --- ### Q2. What is an XML Namespace? Why is it needed? `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **XML Namespace** is a unique string identifier (usually a URI) that is associated with an element or attribute name to **eliminate tag name conflicts** when multiple XML documents (or vocabularies) are combined. **Why needed:** - Different XML vocabularies (e.g., HTML and a custom business vocabulary) may use the **same tag name** for **different purposes** (e.g., `
` for an HTML table vs. a furniture table). - A namespace gives each tag a **globally unique qualified name** of the form `{URI, localName}`. - It allows the **same document** to use tags from multiple sources without ambiguity. **Syntax:** `xmlns:prefix="URI"` declared on any element creates a scope-bound prefix. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`** **(a)** Explain the structure of an XML document with a suitable example. Discuss the various components such as elements, attributes, entities, CDATA sections, and processing instructions. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution (7 marks): **Structure of an XML Document (3 marks):** An XML document consists of: 1. **Prolog** — `` (optional but recommended) 2. **Document Type Declaration** (optional) — links to DTD via `` 3. **Root Element** — exactly one per document, the document element 4. **Content** — nested child elements, text, attributes, etc. **Components Explained (4 marks):** | Component | Purpose | Example | |---|---|---| | **Element** | Primary data container with start tag, content, end tag | `...` | | **Attribute** | Metadata about an element | `` | | **Entity** | Symbolic substitution | `©` for © | | **CDATA Section** | Raw, unparsed text block | `` | | **PI** | Instruction to specific application | `` | | **Comment** | Human note (ignored by parser) | `` | **Full Example Document:** ```xml Web Programming Ramesh K. 499 ``` --- **(b)** Write a valid XML document representing a company organization with departments and employees. Provide an internal DTD that enforces: each department has an id, name, and one or more employees; each employee has name, designation, and salary. The root element is `organization`. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution (7 marks): **Cardinality Analysis (2 marks):** - `organization` → `department+` (1 or more) - `department` → `name`, `employee+` (1 or more) - `employee` → `name`, `designation`, `salary` **Internal DTD + XML Instance (5 marks):** ```xml ]> Software Development Anand Senior Developer 120000 Meera QA Lead 95000 Human Resources Ravi HR Manager 85000 ``` --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`** **(a)** Define XML Namespace. Explain with an example how namespaces resolve tag name conflicts when two XML vocabularies are combined in a single document. Discuss the role of default namespaces. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution (7 marks): **Definition (1 mark):** An **XML Namespace** is a collection of names identified by a **Uniform Resource Identifier (URI)** reference, used as a unique string to qualify element and attribute names and prevent naming collisions. **Conflict Resolution Example (4 marks):** Consider a document that mixes HTML and a custom furniture catalog — both use `
`: ```xml HTML Table Teak 15000 ``` | Prefix | Namespace URI | Resolves `
` to | |---|---|---| | `h` | `http://www.w3.org/TR/html4/` | HTML table semantics | | `f` | `http://www.furniture.com/catalog` | Furniture table semantics | The parser builds the **expanded name** as `{URI}localName`, ensuring **no ambiguity**. **Default Namespaces (2 marks):** - Declared using `xmlns="URI"` (no prefix). - Applies to all **unqualified** elements within the scope of declaration. - Useful when one vocabulary dominates and prefixes are unnecessary. ```xml XML Basics ``` --- **(b)** Explain the major XML vocabularies used in industry. List any five and describe their domain, key features, and one real-world use case for each. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution (7 marks): A **vocabulary** is a domain-specific collection of XML element and attribute names built on top of XML's syntax, defining a custom markup language for a particular industry or function. | # | Vocabulary | Domain | Key Features | Real-World Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **MathML** | Mathematics | Presentation + Content markup; supports equations, matrices, integrals | Rendering math in browsers (Firefox, Safari) | | 2 | **SVG** | Vector Graphics | Scalable, resolution-independent 2D graphics; animation support | Web icons, charts, logos | | 3 | **XHTML** | Web Pages | Stricter, XML-compliant version of HTML | Modern web page serialization | | 4 | **RSS / Atom** | News Syndication | Channel/item-based, standardized for blogs and podcasts | Blog readers, podcast apps | | 5 | **SOAP** | Web Services | XML-based protocol with envelope structure, transport-agnostic | Enterprise B2B communication | | 6 | **DocBook** | Technical Publishing | Standardized tags for books, articles, chapters | O'Reilly, Linux man pages | | 7 | **OOXML** | Office Documents | ZIP-packaged XML for `.docx`, `.xlsx`, `.pptx` | Microsoft Office file formats | **Concluding Note (1 mark):** All these vocabularies share the **common XML foundation** — well-formedness, validation, and namespace rules — demonstrating XML's power as a universal data-interchange meta-language. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > - Forgetting to quote attribute values → **-1 mark** per occurrence. > - Using HTML-style unclosed tags (`
`) → **-1 to -2 marks**. > - Confusing **well-formedness** with **validation** — KTU specifically tests this distinction. > - In DTDs, mixing sequence and choice operators incorrectly — e.g., `(a vert b), c` means "a OR b, then c", not "all of these". → **-2 marks**. > - Using **two root elements** → invalid; document is rejected. → **-2 marks**. > - Forgetting that `ID` attribute values **must start with a letter** — purely numeric IDs fail. → **-1 to -2 marks**. > - In namespace questions, students often write **plain URL** instead of correctly binding a **prefix to a URI** via `xmlns`. → **-2 marks**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **XML = Extensible Markup Language** — meta-language for structuring data, not presentation. - [x] **Three pillars of XML:** well-formedness (syntax), DTD/Schema (validation), Namespaces (uniqueness). - [x] **Well-formed ≠ Valid** — well-formed is *necessary* but *not sufficient*; valid requires DTD/Schema. - [x] **Single root element** is mandatory in every XML document. - [x] **Tags are case-sensitive** — `` and `` are different. - [x] **Attribute values must always be quoted** with `"` or `'`. - [x] **Entity references** are needed for `<`, `>`, `&`, `"`, `'` in text. - [x] **CDATA sections** are the escape hatch for blocks of text with many special characters. - [x] **DTD operators:** `+` (≥1), `*` (≥0), `?` (0 or 1), `,` (sequence), `vert` (choice). - [x] **DTD attribute defaults:** `#REQUIRED`, `#IMPLIED`, `#FIXED "v"`, `"default"`. - [x] **DTD attribute types:** `CDATA`, `ID` (unique), `IDREF` (reference), `NMTOKEN`, enumerated. - [x] **Internal DTD** lives inside ``, **External DTD** uses `SYSTEM "file.dtd"`. - [x] **Namespaces use URIs as opaque unique strings** — they are NOT fetched by the parser. - [x] **Prefix binding:** `xmlns:prefix="URI"` — applies within the element's scope. - [x] **Default namespace:** `xmlns="URI"` — applies to all unqualified elements. - [x] **Vocabularies** are domain-specific XML dialects: MathML, SVG, XHTML, RSS, SOAP, etc. - [x] **Reserved characters in text:** always use `<`, `>`, `&` — never raw. - [x] **Processing instructions (PI)** pass instructions to a specific application (e.g., ``). - [x] **ID values must start with a letter** — `id="101"` is invalid for type `ID`. - [x] **Kerala KTU exam weightage:** Module 1 XML basics typically carries **14–21 marks** in ESE.

topics.title: Making Asynchronous Requests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECST832 - WEB PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Making Asynchronous Requests ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme Web Programming syllabus (OECST832)**, *Making Asynchronous Requests* refers to the client-side technique of communicating with a web server **without blocking the execution thread** of the browser, allowing the Document Object Model (DOM) to remain interactive while data is fetched, sent, or updated in the background. The three canonical technologies covered in Module 2 are: 1. **XMLHttpRequest (XHR)** – the legacy W3C-standard JavaScript object. 2. **Fetch API** – the modern Promise-based replacement (ES6 / WHATWG). 3. **Async/Await** – syntactic sugar over Promises introduced in **ES2017 (ES8)** for linear, readable asynchronous flow. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must be able to *create, send, and handle* asynchronous HTTP requests using both **XMLHttpRequest** and the **Fetch API**, distinguish between **synchronous vs asynchronous** behavior, and parse the response in **JSON** or **plain text** formats. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Order Slip Imagine a restaurant where you place an order at the counter: - **Synchronous Request:** You stand at the counter, stare at the kitchen, and *block the entire counter* until your food arrives. No one else can order. - **Asynchronous Request:** The waiter hands you a **buzzer/pager** and a **token number**. You go back to your table, chat, scroll your phone, and only react *when the buzzer vibrates*. The counter remains free for others. In browser terms, the **buzzer** is the `onreadystatechange` event (XHR) or the `.then()` / `await` resolution (Fetch). The **food** is the HTTP response payload (JSON / XML / text). ### 1.3 Key Terminology (Standard Metrics in **Bold**) | Term | Standard Value / Meaning | |---|---| | `readyState` | Integer from **0 to 4** indicating the lifecycle stage of an XHR request. | | HTTP Status `200` | **OK** – standard success response. | | HTTP Status `404` | **Not Found** – resource missing. | | HTTP Status `500` | **Internal Server Error**. | | MIME Type | `application/json`, `text/html`, `application/xml`. | | Default Timeout (XHR) | **No native timeout** – must be implemented manually via `setTimeout()`. | > [!NOTE] > Asynchronous requests always travel over **HTTP/HTTPS** on **port 80 / 443** respectively, and respect the **Same-Origin Policy** unless CORS headers are explicitly configured by the server. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Synchronous vs Asynchronous Paradigm #### Synchronous Flow (Blocking) The browser executes one statement at a time. A blocking call (e.g., a network request without async) **freezes the JavaScript event loop**, making the page unresponsive. #### Asynchronous Flow (Non-Blocking) The browser *delegates* the network operation to the **browser's networking API (C++)** and immediately returns control to JavaScript. When the response arrives, a callback / Promise resolution is queued in the **Task Queue** and pushed onto the **Call Stack** by the Event Loop. ### 2.2 Lifecycle of an XMLHttpRequest (XHR) The five ready states of the XHR object are governed by a deterministic state machine: | `readyState` | Constant Name | Meaning | |:---:|---|---| | **0** | `UNSENT` | Object created, `open()` not yet called. | | **1** | `OPENED` | `open()` invoked, headers not sent. | | **2** | `HEADERS_RECEIVED` | `send()` invoked, response headers received. | | **3** | `LOADING` | Response body is being downloaded (partial data in `responseText`). | | **4** | `DONE` | Operation complete — success or failure. | > [!IMPORTANT] > In KTU examinations, examiners **love** testing `readyState = 4 && status === 200` as the canonical "success" condition. Memorize this pair. ### 2.3 HTTP Methods Used in Asynchronous Requests | Method | Purpose | Idempotent? | Body Allowed? | |---|---|:---:|:---:| | **GET** | Retrieve data | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (in standards-compliant implementations) | | **POST** | Submit / create data | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | | **PUT** | Update / replace entire resource | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | | **DELETE** | Remove resource | ✅ Yes | ❌ Rarely | ### 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat-Sheet Since asynchronous programming is conceptual, the "formulas" reduce to **decision-condition truths** that govern request handling: $$ \text{Success} \iff (\texttt{xhr.readyState} = 4) \land (\texttt{xhr.status} \geq 200) \land (\texttt{xhr.status} < 300) $$ $$ \text{Content-Type Header} = \frac{\texttt{application/x-www-form-urlencoded}}{\text{(form)} } \;\; \text{or} \;\; \frac{\texttt{application/json}}{\text{(API)}} $$ $$ \text{URL-Encoded Body} = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} \bigl( \texttt{key}_i \,=\, \texttt{encodeURIComponent}(v_i) \, \big) \; , \; \text{joined by `&`} $$ $$ \text{Promise Tri-State} \in \{\;\texttt{pending},\;\texttt{fulfilled},\;\texttt{rejected}\;\} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Asynchronous requests power virtually every modern interactive web feature — live search suggestions (Google), chat applications (WhatsApp Web), live scoreboards, real-time stock tickers, and Single Page Applications (React, Angular, Vue). ### 2.5 The Fetch API — Modern Replacement The `fetch()` function returns a **Promise** that resolves to a `Response` object. Key advantages over XHR: - Built-in **Promise chaining** (`.then()` / `.catch()`). - Cleaner **method chaining** with `Request` objects. - Native support for **Streams API** (chunked transfer). - No silent failures on network errors (returns `response.ok` flag). ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive XHR Walkthrough — `GET` Request The following derivation maps **every conceptual step** of a `GET` request to the XHR state machine. **Step 1 — Object Instantiation** $$ \texttt{const xhr} = \texttt{new XMLHttpRequest();} $$ This places the XHR in `readyState = 0` (UNSENT). **Step 2 — Initialization via `open()`** $$ \texttt{xhr.open}(\texttt{"GET"},\, \texttt{"https://api.example.com/data"},\, \texttt{true}); $$ The third argument `true` declares the request **asynchronous**. Setting it to `false` makes it **synchronous** — a deprecated anti-pattern in KTU-evaluated code. The state transitions to `readyState = 1` (OPENED). **Step 3 — Event Listener Registration** $$ \texttt{xhr.onreadystatechange} = \texttt{() => \{...} \texttt{ \}} $$ The `onreadystatechange` callback fires every time `readyState` mutates (0→1→2→3→4). **Step 4 — Transmission via `send()`** $$ \texttt{xhr.send();} $$ This dispatches the HTTP packet. Transitions to `readyState = 2` (HEADERS_RECEIVED) once headers arrive, then `3` (LOADING) as the body streams in, finally settling at `4` (DONE). **Step 5 — Response Parsing** $$ \texttt{const data} = \texttt{JSON.parse}(\texttt{xhr.responseText}); $$ The `responseText` property is populated incrementally during state `3` and finalized at state `4`. ### 3.2 Exhaustive XHR Walkthrough — `POST` Request with JSON **Step 1 — Instantiate and Open** $$ \texttt{const xhr} = \texttt{new XMLHttpRequest();} \quad \texttt{xhr.open}(\texttt{"POST"},\, \texttt{"/api/login"},\, \texttt{true}); $$ **Step 2 — Set Request Header** $$ \texttt{xhr.setRequestHeader}(\texttt{"Content-Type"},\, \texttt{"application/json"}); $$ Without this header, the server cannot parse the payload correctly. **Step 3 — Construct Payload and Send** $$ \begin{aligned} \texttt{const payload} &= \texttt{JSON.stringify}(\{\texttt{user}:\texttt{"admin"},\, \texttt{pass}:\texttt{"1234"}\}); \\[4pt] \texttt{xhr.send}(\texttt{payload}); \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Handle Resolution** $$ \begin{aligned} \texttt{xhr.onload} &= \texttt{() => \{ } \\ &\quad \texttt{if} \; (\texttt{xhr.status} === 200) \; \{ \texttt{console.log}(\texttt{JSON.parse}(\texttt{xhr.responseText}));\; \texttt{\}} \\ &\quad \texttt{else} \; \{ \texttt{console.error}(\texttt{"HTTP Error:"},\, \texttt{xhr.status});\; \texttt{\}} \\ &\texttt{\};} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.3 Fetch API Walkthrough **Step 1 — Initiate Request** $$ \texttt{fetch}(\texttt{"https://api.example.com/users"}) $$ This returns a `Promise` in the **pending** state. **Step 2 — Read Body (Stream Consumption)** $$ \begin{aligned} \texttt{fetch}(\texttt{"..."}).texttt{then}(\texttt{response} \texttt{ =>} \{ \\ &\quad \texttt{return response.json();} \quad \text{// returns a Promise} \\ &\texttt{\}) \\ \texttt{.then}(\texttt{data} \texttt{ => console.log}(\texttt{data})) \\ \texttt{.catch}(\texttt{err} \texttt{ => console.error}(\texttt{err})); \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > `fetch()` **does not reject** on HTTP errors like 404 or 500. It only rejects on **network failures**. You MUST inspect `response.ok` (boolean) before parsing. ### 3.4 Async/Await Walkthrough **Step 1 — Wrap in `async` Function** $$ \begin{aligned} \texttt{async function getUser}() \; \{ \\ &\quad \texttt{try} \; \{ \\ &\qquad \texttt{const response} = \texttt{await fetch}(\texttt{"/api/user/1"}); \\ &\qquad \texttt{if} \; (!\texttt{response.ok}) \; \texttt{throw new Error}(\texttt{"HTTP "} + \texttt{response.status}); \\ &\qquad \texttt{const data} = \texttt{await response.json();} \\ &\qquad \texttt{console.log}(\texttt{data});} \\ &\quad \texttt{\}} \texttt{ catch} \; (\texttt{err}) \; \{ \texttt{console.error}(\texttt{err}); \texttt{\}} \\ &\texttt{\}} \end{aligned} $$ The `await` keyword **suspends** the async function's execution, allowing the event loop to process other tasks until the Promise settles. ### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Feature | XHR | Fetch API | Async/Await | |---|:---:|:---:|:---:| | Introduced | 2006 (IE7) | 2015 (ES6) | 2017 (ES8) | | Syntax Style | Event-based | Promise-based | Syntactic sugar over Promises | | Streams Support | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ (via Fetch) | | Progress Events | ✅ (`xhr.upload.onprogress`) | ❌ (needs Streams API) | ❌ | | Automatic JSON Parse | ❌ (manual) | ❌ (`response.json()` method) | ❌ | | Network Error Rejection | ❌ (status only) | ✅ (network only) | ✅ (via try/catch) | | Browser Support | Universal | Modern (IE ✗) | Modern (IE ✗) | | KTU Recommendation | **Mandatory** | **Mandatory** | **Recommended** | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Asynchronous Request Flow — Block-Level Functional Architecture The following Mermaid block diagram maps the architectural interactions of an asynchronous HTTP request lifecycle from the user action to server response and DOM update. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[User Interaction
e.g., Button Click] --> B[JavaScript Event Handler
onclick handler invoked] B --> C{Request Method Selection} C -->|GET| D1[Build Query String
URL ? key=value and key2=value2] C -->|POST| D2[Build Request Body
JSON.stringify payload] D1 --> E[Create XHR or Fetch] D2 --> E E --> F[open Method HTTP Verb URL async true] F --> G[setRequestHeader Content-Type application slash json] G --> H[send Dispatch to Browser Networking API] H --> I[Browser C++ Layer Sends HTTP Packet] I --> J[Web Server Processes Request] J --> K{Response Received} K -->|Success| L[readyState 4 status 200] K -->|Client Error| M[readyState 4 status 4xx] K -->|Server Error| N[readyState 4 status 5xx] K -->|Network Failure| O[onerror fired or Promise rejected] L --> P[JSON.parse responseText] M --> P N --> P O --> Q[Log error to console or UI] P --> R[Update DOM with response data] R --> S[Re-render affected UI components] Q --> S S --> T[Browser Event Loop Idle Awaiting Next Task] ``` ### 4.2 XHR State Machine — Lifecycle Diagram ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> UNSENT UNSENT --> OPENED : open method called OPENED --> HEADERS_RECEIVED : send called + response headers arrive HEADERS_RECEIVED --> LOADING : response body streaming LOADING --> DONE : full body received or error DONE --> [*] ``` ### 4.3 Promise Resolution Flow — Fetch API ```mermaid flowchart LR A[fetch URL invoked] --> B[Promise in pending state] B --> C{Network Result} C -->|Resolved| D[Promise fulfilled with Response object] C -->|Network Failure| E[Promise rejected with TypeError] D --> F{response ok check} F -->|true| G[await response.json method] F -->|false| H[Throw new Error HTTP status] G --> I[Data ready for DOM update] H --> J[catch block executes] E --> J I --> K[End of async function] J --> K ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading Guide for the Diagrams:** The labeled boxes (`A`, `B`, `C`, etc.) represent system states; the arrows represent the direction of data flow or control transfer. The diamond boxes are conditional decision nodes. The terminal boxes (`T`, `K`) represent the resting state awaiting the next user interaction. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define asynchronous requests in web programming. List any two advantages of using the Fetch API over XMLHttpRequest.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** *(1 mark)* Asynchronous requests are HTTP calls initiated from the client-side JavaScript that **do not block the main execution thread**, allowing the browser to remain responsive while the server processes the request and returns a response. *(1 mark)* **Advantage 1:** The Fetch API uses Promises, enabling cleaner `.then()/.catch()` chaining and avoiding deeply nested callbacks (callback hell). *(1 mark)* **Advantage 2:** It natively supports modern features like the **Streams API** for chunked data transfer and integrates seamlessly with `async/await` syntax for linear, readable code. --- #### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Explain the five `readyState` values of an XMLHttpRequest object with their corresponding constant names.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** *(½ mark each)* | Value | Constant | Meaning | |:---:|---|---| | **0** | `UNSENT` | Object created, `open()` not yet called. | | **1** | `OPENED` | `open()` invoked successfully. | | **2** | `HEADERS_RECEIVED` | `send()` called, response headers available. | | **3** | `LOADING` | Response body is being downloaded. | | **4** | `DONE` | Operation complete (success or failure). | --- ### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Internal Choice Instruction (Per KTU Pattern):** *Answer ANY ONE full question from the pair.* --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Write a complete JavaScript program using **XMLHttpRequest** to send a `GET` request to the URL `https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users/1` and display the user's name and email in an HTML page with `
`. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the difference between **synchronous** and **asynchronous** requests. Illustrate with a real-world example. **(7 marks)** --- ### 🔹 Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks) **Conceptual Setup (2 marks):** - Create an XHR object using `new XMLHttpRequest()`. - Use `open("GET", url, true)` to initialize the asynchronous GET request. **State & Send Logic (2 marks):** - Attach an `onreadystatechange` listener that checks `readyState === 4 && xhr.status === 200`. - Invoke `xhr.send()` to dispatch the request. **DOM Update Logic (2 marks):** - Parse the response with `JSON.parse(xhr.responseText)`. - Inject `data.name` and `data.email` into `
` via `innerHTML`. **Complete Program (1 mark):** ```html XHR GET Demo
Loading...
``` ### 🔹 Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks) **Definition (2 marks):** - **Synchronous Request:** The browser sends an HTTP request and **blocks (freezes) the JavaScript event loop** until the response arrives. The user interface becomes unresponsive. - **Asynchronous Request:** The browser sends the HTTP request and **immediately returns control** to the script. The UI stays interactive. A callback (or Promise) is invoked when the response eventually arrives. **Operational Difference (2 marks):** - Synchronous: `xhr.open(method, url, false)` — the third parameter is `false`. - Asynchronous: `xhr.open(method, url, true)` — the third parameter is `true`. This is the default in modern JavaScript. **Real-World Example (2 marks):** Consider a payment confirmation page. In a **synchronous** flow, clicking "Pay" freezes the screen with a white blank page for several seconds — the user may click again, causing duplicate charges. In an **asynchronous** flow, the user sees a loading spinner, can scroll, and is notified only when the payment gateway responds. **Diagrammatic Comparison (1 mark):** ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SYNC[Synchronous Flow] S1[JS sends request] --> S2[Event loop BLOCKED] S2 --> S3[Response arrives] S3 --> S4[UI unfreezes] end subgraph ASYNC[Asynchronous Flow] A1[JS sends request] --> A2[Event loop FREE] A2 --> A3[UI remains responsive] A3 --> A4[Callback fires on response] end ``` --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Write a complete JavaScript program using the **Fetch API with async/await** to POST a JSON payload `{ "title": "foo", "body": "bar", "userId": 1 }` to `https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts` and log the server's response. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare XMLHttpRequest and Fetch API in terms of (i) error handling, (ii) stream support, and (iii) progress events. **(7 marks)** --- ### 🔹 Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks) **Function Definition (1 mark):** - Define an `async function postData()`. **Fetch Call (2 marks):** - Call `fetch(url, { method: "POST", headers: {...}, body: JSON.stringify(payload) })`. - Set `Content-Type: application/json` in headers. **Async/Await Handling (2 marks):** - `const response = await fetch(...)`. - Check `if (!response.ok) throw new Error("HTTP " + response.status)`. - `const data = await response.json()`. **Try/Catch & Logging (2 marks):** - Wrap in `try/catch` to handle network errors. - `console.log(data)` on success. **Complete Program:** ```javascript async function postData() { const url = "https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts"; const payload = { title: "foo", body: "bar", userId: 1 }; try { const response = await fetch(url, { method: "POST", headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" }, body: JSON.stringify(payload) }); if (!response.ok) { throw new Error("HTTP Error: " + response.status); } const data = await response.json(); console.log("Server Response:", data); } catch (err) { console.error("Request failed:", err); } } postData(); ``` ### 🔹 Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks) | Comparison Axis | XMLHttpRequest (XHR) | Fetch API | Marks | |---|---|---|:---:| | **(i) Error Handling** | Status code must be checked manually (e.g., `xhr.status === 200`); no automatic rejection on HTTP errors. | `fetch()` only rejects on **network failures**; HTTP errors (404, 500) require `response.ok` check. | **(2)** | | **(ii) Stream Support** | Does not support streaming; full response buffered. | Natively supports the **Streams API** via `response.body.getReader()` for chunked consumption. | **(2)** | | **(iii) Progress Events** | Provides `xhr.upload.onprogress` and `xhr.onprogress` for granular upload/download tracking. | No built-in progress events; requires manual implementation using the Streams API. | **(2)** | | **Conclusion** | Best for legacy support and progress tracking. | Best for modern applications with `async/await`. | **(1)** | --- > [!WARNING] > **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Forgetting the third parameter in `xhr.open()`:** Students often write `xhr.open("GET", url)` — the default is `true` (async), but examiners expect you to **explicitly state it** for clarity. *(Loss: 0.5 – 1 mark)* > 2. **Not checking `response.ok` in Fetch API:** A `fetch()` call returning a 404 or 500 status **does NOT throw**. Students who omit `response.ok` checks and assume success lose full method marks. *(Loss: up to 2 marks)* > 3. **Confusing `onload` with `onreadystatechange`:** `onload` fires only once at `readyState = 4`, whereas `onreadystatechange` fires for **every** state transition. Mentioning the wrong one in viva costs marks. *(Loss: 1 mark)* > 4. **Synchronous XMLHttpRequest in code:** Writing `xhr.open(..., false)` is a deprecated anti-pattern. KTU examiners deduct marks for poor practices. *(Loss: 1–2 marks)* --- ### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Asynchronous requests** do **not block** the event loop; the UI stays responsive. - ✅ The **XHR lifecycle** has 5 states (0–4); the success condition is `readyState === 4 && status === 200`. - ✅ The `Fetch API` returns a **Promise** that resolves to a `Response` object. - ✅ `fetch()` **rejects ONLY on network errors** — HTTP errors require `response.ok` verification. - ✅ `async/await` is **syntactic sugar** over Promises; it does not replace them. - ✅ `JSON.parse(xhr.responseText)` converts a JSON string to a JavaScript object. - ✅ `JSON.stringify(obj)` converts a JavaScript object to a JSON string (used in POST bodies). - ✅ For POST requests, always set `Content-Type: application/json` via `setRequestHeader` (XHR) or `headers` option (Fetch). - ✅ HTTP **GET** is for retrieval; **POST** is for submission. GET should not have a body. - ✅ The `onprogress` event exists in XHR (for progress bars) but **not** in Fetch API. - ✅ CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) must be configured on the server to allow cross-domain asynchronous requests. - ✅ KTU expects you to know **both** XHR (legacy mandatory) and Fetch (modern mandatory) — do not skip either.

topics.title: Complete Control Over AJAX
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST832 - WEB PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Complete Control Over AJAX

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Complete Control Over AJAX** refers to the mastery of the low-level `XMLHttpRequest (XHR)` object — the foundational engine of every asynchronous web request. In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, it denotes the programmer's ability to programmatically dictate **every phase** of the HTTP request/response lifecycle: instantiation, configuration, transmission, state monitoring, header manipulation, response parsing, error handling, and termination.

According to the KTU 2024 Web Programming syllabus (Module 2), *Complete Control Over AJAX* is the in-depth study of:
1. The `XMLHttpRequest` object architecture.
2. The HTTP request lifecycle (`open()` → `setRequestHeader()` → `send()`).
3. The five `readyState` values and their semantic meaning.
4. HTTP response codes and header introspection.
5. Synchronous vs. Asynchronous request execution models.
6. Server-side processing of AJAX requests (PHP handler side).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** "Complete control over the `XMLHttpRequest` object" is the *exact phrasing* used in the KTU Web Programming (OECST832) Module 2 syllabus. Expect direct 7–14 mark questions on the lifecycle, methods, properties, and readyState transitions of this object.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Restaurant Order System 🍽️**

Imagine you are sitting in a restaurant:

| Restaurant Action | AJAX Equivalent |
|---|---|
| You sit at the table (without leaving) | Browser stays on the same page (no reload) |
| You call the waiter | `xhr.open(method, url, async)` |
| You tell the waiter your exact order | `xhr.send(data)` |
| The waiter takes the order to the kitchen | Request travels to the server |
| The waiter periodically checks kitchen status | `onreadystatechange` event fires repeatedly |
| Food arrives at different prep stages | `readyState` changes from 0 → 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 |
| Final dish is served to your table | `responseText` or `responseXML` delivered to JS |
| The waiter reports "Out of stock!" | HTTP `status === 404` or `500` |

**"Complete Control"** means you are not just ordering — you are the **restaurant manager** who decides *what gets sent, when it gets sent, how to check its status, and what to do at every stage*.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

- **`readyState`**: An integer in the range **0 to 4** (5 possible values).
- **HTTP Status Codes**: Range from **100 to 599**, grouped into 5 classes.
- **Asynchronous Flag**: Boolean (`true` = asynchronous, `false` = synchronous — *deprecated for main thread*).
- **W3C Standardization**: `XMLHttpRequest` became a **W3C Working Draft** recommendation in **2006** and is now universally supported.

## 1.4 Module 2 Context

In KTU's Web Programming OECST832, **Module 2 (AJAX)** typically covers:
- Introduction to AJAX
- The `XMLHttpRequest` object
- **Complete Control Over AJAX** ← *this topic*
- AJAX with PHP/MySQL server-side
- AJAX with JSON
- AJAX with XML

This topic is the **technical backbone** of Module 2 — every subsequent AJAX concept (database integration, JSON parsing) builds on the lifecycle control discussed here.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The XMLHttpRequest Object — Anatomy

The `XMLHttpRequest` (XHR) object is the **core API** that allows JavaScript to make HTTP requests to a server *without* reloading the entire page. To achieve "complete control," you must master its **properties** (read state) and **methods** (issue commands).

### 2.1.1 Object Creation (Cross-Browser Safe)

```javascript
// Modern browsers (IE7+, all modern engines)
var xhr;
if (window.XMLHttpRequest) {
    xhr = new XMLHttpRequest();         // Standard
} else {
    xhr = new ActiveXObject("Microsoft.XMLHTTP"); // Legacy IE5/6
}
```

## 2.2 Methods of XMLHttpRequest — The Command Set

| Method | Syntax | Purpose | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| `open()` | `xhr.open(method, URL, async, user, pass)` | Initializes a request. **Does not send it.** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| `send()` | `xhr.send(body)` | Transmits the request. `body` is `null` for GET. | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| `setRequestHeader()` | `xhr.setRequestHeader(label, value)` | Adds a custom HTTP header (must be called *after* `open()` and *before* `send()`). | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| `getResponseHeader()` | `xhr.getResponseHeader("Content-Type")` | Returns a specific response header as a string. | ⭐⭐ |
| `getAllResponseHeaders()` | `xhr.getAllResponseHeaders()` | Returns all response headers as a single string. | ⭐⭐ |
| `abort()` | `xhr.abort()` | Cancels the current request; resets `readyState` to 0. | ⭐⭐⭐ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Rule of `open()` and `send()`:** You **cannot** send a request without first calling `open()`. The XHR object is a *two-stage launcher* — configure, then fire.

### 2.2.1 The `open()` Method — Parameters Decoded

| Parameter | Type | Description | Typical Values |
|---|---|---|---|
| `method` | String | HTTP verb | `"GET"`, `"POST"`, `"PUT"`, `"DELETE"` |
| `URL` | String | Server endpoint | `"server.php"`, `"https://api.example.com/data"` |
| `async` | Boolean | Asynchronous flag | `true` (99% of the time), `false` (legacy) |
| `user` | String (opt) | HTTP auth username | `"admin"` |
| `pass` | String (opt) | HTTP auth password | `"secret123"` |

## 2.3 Properties of XMLHttpRequest — The Sensor Array

| Property | Type | Description | Key Insight |
|---|---|---|---|
| `onreadystatechange` | Function | Event handler fired on **every** `readyState` change. | Store as a function reference, not a string. |
| `readyState` | Integer | Current state of the request (0–4). | 4 = "Done" |
| `status` | Integer | HTTP status code returned by server (e.g., 200, 404). | Only reliable when `readyState === 4` |
| `statusText` | String | HTTP status text (e.g., `"OK"`, `"Not Found"`). | Human-readable companion to `status` |
| `responseText` | String | Raw response body as a plain string. | Always populated when `readyState === 4` |
| `responseXML` | Document | Response body parsed as an XML DOM (only if `Content-Type: text/xml`). | `null` for JSON or plain text |
| `responseType` | String | Expected response type. | `"text"`, `"json"`, `"document"`, `"arraybuffer"`, `"blob"` |
| `withCredentials` | Boolean | Whether to send cookies/auth headers cross-origin. | CORS-related |
| `timeout` | Integer | Milliseconds before auto-abort. | Default is 0 (no timeout) |
| `upload` | Object | Progress tracking for uploads. | Used in file upload scenarios |

## 2.4 The `readyState` Lifecycle — The Heart of AJAX Control

> [!NOTE]
> The `readyState` property is the **single most important concept** in AJAX. KTU board exams *frequently* test it with full 14-mark questions.

| Value | State Name | Description | Trigger |
|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | `UNSENT` | Client created, but `open()` not yet called. | `new XMLHttpRequest()` |
| **1** | `OPENED` | `open()` has been called. Request is configured. | `xhr.open(...)` |
| **2** | `HEADERS_RECEIVED` | `send()` called; response **headers** and **status** are available. | Server has responded with headers |
| **3** | `LOADING` | Response **body** is being received (downloading). | Partial data available in `responseText` |
| **4** | `DONE` | Operation **complete**. All data received. | Final state — check `status` and `responseText` |

### 2.4.1 Visual Flow of `readyState`

```
0 (UNSENT)
   │  xhr.open(...)
   ▼
1 (OPENED)
   │  xhr.setRequestHeader(...)
   │  xhr.send(...)
   ▼
2 (HEADERS_RECEIVED)
   │  Server returns headers
   ▼
3 (LOADING)
   │  Response body downloading
   ▼
4 (DONE) ──► Check status === 200 ──► Use responseText / responseXML
```

## 2.5 HTTP Status Codes — The Server's Verdict

| Code Class | Range | Meaning | Common Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1xx** | 100–199 | Informational | `100 Continue` |
| **2xx** | 200–299 | **Success** | `200 OK`, `201 Created` |
| **3xx** | 300–399 | Redirection | `301 Moved Permanently`, `304 Not Modified` |
| **4xx** | 400–499 | **Client Error** | `404 Not Found`, `403 Forbidden`, `401 Unauthorized` |
| **5xx** | 500–599 | **Server Error** | `500 Internal Server Error`, `503 Service Unavailable` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Best Practice Pattern:** Always check `if (xhr.readyState === 4 && xhr.status === 200)` before processing the response. The `&&` is **not optional** — checking status while still loading (readyState 3) will give you partial data and incorrect error handling.

## 2.6 Synchronous vs. Asynchronous — Engineering Trade-off

| Aspect | Asynchronous (`async = true`) | Synchronous (`async = false`) |
|---|---|---|
| UI Blocking | **No** — page remains responsive | **Yes** — page freezes until response |
| Event Handler | `onreadystatechange` used | `xhr.send()` returns *after* completion |
| Modern Web Standard | ✅ Recommended | ❌ Deprecated (still works, but discouraged) |
| User Experience | Smooth, no "jank" | Frozen page = bad UX |
| W3C Recommendation | Strongly preferred | Removed from main thread spec |

> [!WARNING]
> **Synchronous XHR on the main thread is being removed from the web platform.** Use `async = true` and the `onreadystatechange` (or modern `onload`/`onerror`) handlers in all KTU submissions.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The "Complete Control Over AJAX" pattern is the foundation of:

1. **Live Search Autocomplete** (Google Search, e-commerce filter bars)
2. **Infinite Scroll** feeds (Twitter, Facebook, Instagram)
3. **Real-time Dashboards** (Stock tickers, monitoring systems)
4. **Form Validation** without page reload (sign-up form username availability)
5. **Single Page Applications (SPAs)** — React, Angular, Vue all evolved from this exact lifecycle
6. **E-commerce Cart Updates** (add to cart → page doesn't reload)

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Summary Table)

| Concept | Key Value / Pattern | Memory Hook |
|---|---|---|
| `readyState` range | **0–4** | "0 is unborn, 4 is done" |
| Success state condition | `readyState === 4 && status === 200` | "4 and 200 — the magic duo" |
| `open()` order | First, before `send()` | "Open the door before you send" |
| `setRequestHeader` timing | After `open()`, before `send()` | "Header sandwich" |
| GET request data location | URL query string | "GET goes in the URL" |
| POST request data location | `send(body)` argument | "POST goes in the body" |
| `responseXML` condition | Server must send `Content-Type: text/xml` or `application/xml` | "XML needs XML headers" |
| Abort a request | `xhr.abort()` | "Pull the plug" |
| Asynchronous default | `true` | "Async is the modern way" |

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs & Code Derivations

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The Canonical "Complete Control" Pattern

The following derivation walks through the **complete, textbook-quality** implementation of a "Complete Control Over AJAX" workflow, as expected in KTU 14-mark answers.

### Step 1 — Instantiate the XHR Object

```javascript
var xhr = new XMLHttpRequest();
```

**Logic:** We create the XHR object. At this moment, `readyState === 0` (UNSENT). The object exists in memory but has not been configured.

### Step 2 — Define the State-Change Handler

```javascript
xhr.onreadystatechange = function() {
    if (xhr.readyState === 4 && xhr.status === 200) {
        document.getElementById("result").innerHTML = xhr.responseText;
    } else if (xhr.readyState === 4 && xhr.status !== 200) {
        document.getElementById("result").innerHTML = "Error: " + xhr.status;
    }
};
```

**Logic:** This anonymous function will be called *automatically* by the browser **every time** `readyState` changes (states 1, 2, 3, 4). We only act on **state 4** (DONE) and then check the HTTP status.

### Step 3 — Configure the Request with `open()`

```javascript
xhr.open("GET", "server.php?name=John&age=25", true);
```

**Logic:**
- Method: `GET` — data will travel in the URL.
- URL: `server.php` — the server-side handler.
- `async = true` — non-blocking, recommended.

After this call, `readyState === 1` (OPENED).

### Step 4 — (Optional) Set Custom Headers

```javascript
xhr.setRequestHeader("Content-Type", "application/x-www-form-urlencoded");
xhr.setRequestHeader("X-Requested-With", "XMLHttpRequest");
```

**Logic:** Headers must be added *between* `open()` and `send()`. The `X-Requested-With: XMLHttpRequest` header is a **server-side convention** used by PHP's `$_SERVER['HTTP_X_REQUESTED_WITH']` to detect AJAX requests.

### Step 5 — Transmit with `send()`

```javascript
xhr.send();                  // For GET (no body)
xhr.send("name=John&age=25"); // For POST (body contains data)
```

**Logic:** `send()` initiates the HTTP request. After this call, `readyState` immediately advances to **2** (HEADERS_RECEIVED) once the server replies, then **3** (LOADING), and finally **4** (DONE).

### Step 6 — Process the Response

Once `readyState === 4 && status === 200`:
- `xhr.responseText` contains the raw text response.
- `xhr.responseXML` contains the parsed XML DOM (if applicable).
- Data can be injected into the DOM via `innerHTML`, `textContent`, or by parsing JSON.

## 3.2 Complete GET Request — Full Annotated Example

```javascript
// Step 1: Create the object
var xhr = new XMLHttpRequest();

// Step 2: Define the response handler
xhr.onreadystatechange = function() {
    // readyState 4 = DONE, status 200 = OK
    if (xhr.readyState === 4) {
        if (xhr.status === 200) {
            document.getElementById("output").innerHTML = xhr.responseText;
        } else {
            alert("Request failed with status: " + xhr.status);
        }
    }
};

// Step 3: Open a GET request
xhr.open("GET", "getuser.php?id=101", true);

// Step 4: Send it
xhr.send();
```

## 3.3 Complete POST Request — Full Annotated Example

```javascript
var xhr = new XMLHttpRequest();
xhr.onreadystatechange = function() {
    if (xhr.readyState === 4 && xhr.status === 200) {
        var response = JSON.parse(xhr.responseText);
        console.log(response.message);
    }
};

xhr.open("POST", "saveuser.php", true);
xhr.setRequestHeader("Content-Type", "application/x-www-form-urlencoded");
xhr.send("username=alice&email=alice@example.com");
```

**Logic Notes:**
- POST data travels in the **body**, not the URL.
- A `Content-Type` header **must** be set for the server to interpret the body.
- `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` is the default for HTML forms.

## 3.4 ReadyState Transition — Derivation Table

| Step | Action Called | New `readyState` | What's Available |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `new XMLHttpRequest()` | **0** | Nothing yet |
| 2 | `xhr.open(method, url, true)` | **1** | Request configured; nothing sent |
| 3 | `xhr.setRequestHeader(...)` | **1** | Still OPENED (headers pending) |
| 4 | `xhr.send(...)` | **1→2** | After server replies with status line and headers |
| 5 | Server sends body | **2→3** | `responseText` growing as data streams in |
| 6 | Transfer complete | **3→4** | `responseText` final; `status` and `statusText` set |

## 3.5 JSON vs XML Response Handling — Comparative Derivation

**Server returns JSON** (`Content-Type: application/json`):
```javascript
if (xhr.readyState === 4 && xhr.status === 200) {
    var data = JSON.parse(xhr.responseText);
    document.getElementById("name").innerHTML = data.name;
    document.getElementById("age").innerHTML = data.age;
}
```

**Server returns XML** (`Content-Type: text/xml`):
```javascript
if (xhr.readyState === 4 && xhr.status === 200) {
    var xmlDoc = xhr.responseXML; // Auto-parsed by browser
    var name = xmlDoc.getElementsByTagName("name")[0].childNodes[0].nodeValue;
    document.getElementById("name").innerHTML = name;
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:** `responseXML` is **only** populated when the server's `Content-Type` header is XML. If the server returns XML with `Content-Type: text/plain`, the browser will *not* parse it, and `responseXML` will be `null`. This is a common KTU exam trick question.

## 3.6 Error Handling — Defensive Pattern

```javascript
xhr.onreadystatechange = function() {
    if (xhr.readyState === 4) {
        if (xhr.status === 200) {
            // Success
            processResponse(xhr.responseText);
        } else if (xhr.status === 404) {
            alert("Resource not found");
        } else if (xhr.status === 500) {
            alert("Server error — contact admin");
        } else {
            alert("Unexpected status: " + xhr.status);
        }
    }
};
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note the `else if` cascade on `readyState === 4`:** We don't repeat `readyState === 4` in each branch — it's pulled out into the parent `if`. This is the **idiomatic KTU-expected pattern** for clean AJAX error handling.

## 3.7 Aborting a Request — Cancellation Pattern

```javascript
var xhr = new XMLHttpRequest();
xhr.open("GET", "slow-server.php", true);
xhr.send();

// User clicks "Cancel" button after 2 seconds
setTimeout(function() {
    xhr.abort(); // Cancels the request
    console.log("Request aborted at readyState:", xhr.readyState); // 0
}, 2000);
```

**Logic:** `abort()` resets the XHR object back to `readyState === 0` (UNSENT), making it reusable for a new request.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete AJAX Request-Response Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Action on Web Page] --> B[JavaScript Event Handler]
    B --> C["xhr = new XMLHttpRequest()"]
    C --> D["xhr.onreadystatechange = function()"]
    D --> E["xhr.open(method, url, async)"]
    E --> F{Async?}
    F -->|true| G[Non-Blocking - Browser Continues]
    F -->|false| H[Blocking - Page Freezes]
    G --> I["xhr.setRequestHeader() (optional)"]
    H --> I
    I --> J["xhr.send(data)"]
    J --> K[readyState = 1 - OPENED]
    K --> L[HTTP Request Travels to Server]
    L --> M[Server Script e.g. PHP]
    M --> N[Database Query or Processing]
    N --> O[Server Sends HTTP Response]
    O --> P[readyState = 2 - HEADERS RECEIVED]
    P --> Q[readyState = 3 - LOADING]
    Q --> R[readyState = 4 - DONE]
    R --> S{status === 200?}
    S -->|Yes| T[Use responseText or responseXML]
    S -->|No| U[Handle Error by status code]
    T --> V[Update DOM innerHTML, textContent]
    U --> W[Show Error Message to User]
    V --> X[End - User Sees Updated Content]
    W --> X
```

## 4.2 The `readyState` State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> UNSENT: new XMLHttpRequest()
    UNSENT --> OPENED: xhr.open()
    OPENED --> OPENED: xhr.setRequestHeader()
    OPENED --> HEADERS_RECEIVED: xhr.send() + server reply
    HEADERS_RECEIVED --> LOADING: body streaming
    LOADING --> DONE: transfer complete
    DONE --> [*]: check status
    OPENED --> UNSENT: xhr.abort()
    LOADING --> UNSENT: xhr.abort()
```

## 4.3 Method Invocation Sequence (Sequence Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Browser
    participant JS as JavaScript Engine
    participant XHR as XMLHttpRequest
    participant SV as Server PHP

    U->>JS: Click button / trigger event
    JS->>XHR: new XMLHttpRequest()
    Note over XHR: readyState = 0
    JS->>XHR: onreadystatechange = handler
    JS->>XHR: open("GET", "url", true)
    Note over XHR: readyState = 1
    JS->>XHR: setRequestHeader("Content-Type", ...)
    JS->>XHR: send(data)
    Note over XHR: readyState = 2
    XHR->>SV: HTTP Request
    SV-->>XHR: HTTP Response Headers
    Note over XHR: readyState = 2
    SV-->>XHR: Response Body (streaming)
    Note over XHR: readyState = 3
    SV-->>XHR: Body complete
    Note over XHR: readyState = 4
    XHR-->>JS: onreadystatechange fires
    JS->>JS: Check status === 200
    JS->>U: Update DOM with responseText
```

## 4.4 Property-Method-Purpose Matrix (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Methods ["COMMAND LAYER - Methods"]
        M1["open() - Configure"]
        M2["send() - Transmit"]
        M3["setRequestHeader() - Add Header"]
        M4["abort() - Cancel"]
        M5["getResponseHeader() - Read Header"]
    end

    subgraph Properties ["SENSOR LAYER - Properties"]
        P1["onreadystatechange - Event Hook"]
        P2["readyState - 0 to 4"]
        P3["status - HTTP Code"]
        P4["responseText - String Body"]
        P5["responseXML - XML DOM"]
    end

    M1 --> P2
    M2 --> P2
    M3 --> M2
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P2 --> P4
    P2 --> P5
```

## 4.5 GET vs POST — Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start[Choose HTTP Method] --> Q{Need to send large<br/>or sensitive data?}
    Q -->|No| GET["GET Method<br/>xhr.open('GET', 'url?key=value', true)"]
    Q -->|Yes| POST["POST Method<br/>xhr.open('POST', 'url', true)<br/>xhr.setRequestHeader(...)<br/>xhr.send('key=value')"]

    GET --> G1[Data in URL]
    POST --> P1[Data in Body]

    G1 --> G2[Bookmarked / Cached]
    P1 --> P2[Not Cached / More Secure]

    G2 --> End[Request Sent]
    P2 --> End
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the five `readyState` values of the `XMLHttpRequest` object with their meanings.**

**Model Answer:**

The `readyState` property indicates the current state of the XMLHttpRequest. It has five possible values:

| Value | State | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | UNSENT | Object is created but `open()` not called |
| 1 | OPENED | `open()` has been called |
| 2 | HEADERS_RECEIVED | `send()` called; response headers and status available |
| 3 | LOADING | Response body is being received |
| 4 | DONE | Operation complete; all data available |

> **(3 marks: 1 for listing + 1 for description + 1 for table/diagram)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between `responseText` and `responseXML` properties of XHR.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `responseText` | `responseXML` |
|---|---|---|
| Data type | String | XML DOM Document |
| Server Content-Type | Any (`text/plain`, `application/json`, etc.) | Must be `text/xml` or `application/xml` |
| Parsing | Manual (e.g., `JSON.parse()`) | Automatic by browser |
| Use case | JSON, plain text, HTML fragments | XML-based APIs |
| Null possibility | Never (if `readyState === 4`) | Can be `null` if Content-Type is not XML |

> **(3 marks: comparison + 1 example each)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the complete lifecycle of an `XMLHttpRequest` object with a suitable diagram. Discuss the role of `onreadystatechange` event handler. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete JavaScript program to send an asynchronous POST request to a PHP file `saveuser.php` with parameters `name` and `email`, and display the server's response in a `<div id="result">` element. Also explain `setRequestHeader()` and why it is required for POST. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

The lifecycle of an `XMLHttpRequest` object consists of **five states**, managed through the `readyState` property:

1. **State 0 (UNSENT):** The XHR object is created using `new XMLHttpRequest()`. No method has been called yet.
2. **State 1 (OPENED):** The `open(method, URL, async)` method is called. The request is *configured* but not yet sent.
3. **State 2 (HEADERS_RECEIVED):** The `send()` method is called. The browser has received the HTTP response headers and the status code (e.g., 200).
4. **State 3 (LOADING):** The response body is being downloaded. `responseText` contains partial data.
5. **State 4 (DONE):** The transfer is complete. `responseText` and `responseXML` are fully populated.

**Role of `onreadystatechange`:**
It is an **event handler** that the browser invokes *automatically* every time `readyState` changes (states 1 → 2 → 3 → 4). Inside this handler, we typically:
- Check `if (xhr.readyState === 4 && xhr.status === 200)` to confirm success.
- Process `xhr.responseText` or `xhr.responseXML`.
- Inject the result into the DOM.

**Diagram:**

```
new XMLHttpRequest()  →  readyState = 0
        │
        ▼
   xhr.open(...)        →  readyState = 1
        │
        ▼
   xhr.send(...)        →  readyState = 2 → 3 → 4
        │
        ▼
   onreadystatechange fires → Process response
```

> **(7 marks: 2 lifecycle + 2 onreadystatechange + 2 diagram + 1 conclusion)**

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**JavaScript Code:**

```javascript
function sendUserData() {
    // Step 1: Instantiate XHR
    var xhr = new XMLHttpRequest();

    // Step 2: Define state change handler
```
