HTML Template"] V1["ng-repeat over students"] V2["ng-model bound inputs"] V3["Event directives ng-click"] end subgraph CTRL["CONTROLLER - JS Logic"] C1["$scope.students array"] C2["$scope.addStudent method"] C3["$scope.removeStudent method"] end subgraph MODEL["MODEL - Data State"] M1["students collection"] M2["newStudent object"] M3["searchText filter"] end subgraph SVC["SERVICES LAYER"] S1["$http for API calls"] S2["Custom Services Singletons"] end VIEW -- "reads / writes" --> CTRL CTRL -- "manages" --> MODEL CTRL -- "calls" --> SVC SVC -- "updates" --> MODEL MODEL -- "triggers digest" --> VIEW style VIEW fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003d7a style CTRL fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806600 style MODEL fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5a2d style SVC fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#5a2d5a ``` ### 4.3 Digest Cycle — State Transition Diagram ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Bootstrap: ng-app parsed Bootstrap --> Idle: Initial render Idle --> Watch: User event or $http response Watch --> DirtyCheck: $digest triggered DirtyCheck --> Stable: No model changes Stable --> Idle DirtyCheck --> UpdateDOM: Model changed UpdateDOM --> DirtyCheck: Recheck until stable DirtyCheck --> Error: More than 10 iterations Error --> [*] ``` ### 4.4 Module-Controller-Service Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A["angular.module 'myApp'"] --> B["controller 'MainCtrl'"] A --> C["service 'DataService'"] A --> D["directive 'myTag'"] A --> E["filter 'myFilter'"] A --> F["config - routing"] B -.uses DI.-> C B -.uses DI.-> G["$http service"] C -.uses DI.-> G style A fill:#ffcc66,stroke:#806600 style B fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003d7a style C fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5a2d style D fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#5a2d5a style E fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806600 style F fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#5a2d2d style G fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#003d7a ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define Single Page Application. List any two advantages of SPA over traditional web applications. **Model Answer:** A **Single Page Application (SPA)** is a web application that loads a single HTML document and dynamically updates its content using JavaScript and AJAX calls, without requiring full page reloads during navigation. **Advantages (any two):** 1. **Faster navigation** — only data is fetched, not full HTML, reducing server load and bandwidth. 2. **Better user experience** — smooth, desktop-like transitions with no page flicker. 3. **Easier state management** — client-side state persists across views. 4. **Separation of concerns** — clear front-end/back-end split via REST APIs. *(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for two distinct advantages.)* --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is two-way data binding in AngularJS? Write the directive used to achieve it. **Model Answer:** **Two-way data binding** is a feature in AngularJS where any change in the model (JavaScript variable) automatically updates the view (HTML), and any change in the view (user input) automatically updates the model. AngularJS keeps both in sync via its **digest cycle**. **Directive used:** `ng-model` **Example:** ```html

Hello, {{ username }}

``` Typing in the input box updates `{{ username }}` in real time without writing any event listener. --- ### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – July 2024] **(a)** Explain the architecture of AngularJS with a neat diagram. Describe the roles of **Model**, **View**, and **Controller** with suitable code examples. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain **directives** in AngularJS. List and briefly describe any **six built-in directives** with one-line examples. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):** AngularJS follows the **MVC/MVW (Model–View–ViewModel/Whatever)** architectural pattern, which separates the application into three interconnected components. | Component | Role | Implementation | |---|---|---| | **Model** | Holds application data (state) | Properties on `$scope` | | **View** | The HTML template rendered to the user | HTML with Angular directives | | **Controller** | Business logic, manipulates Model | JS function registered via `.controller()` | **Code Example — Student Grade Manager:** ```javascript var app = angular.module('gradeApp', []); app.controller('GradeCtrl', function($scope) { // MODEL $scope.student = { name: 'Anand', marks: 0 }; $scope.grade = ''; // BUSINESS LOGIC $scope.computeGrade = function() { var m = $scope.student.marks; if (m >= 90) $scope.grade = 'A+'; else if (m >= 80) $scope.grade = 'A'; else if (m >= 70) $scope.grade = 'B'; else if (m >= 60) $scope.grade = 'C'; else $scope.grade = 'F'; }; }); ``` **View:** ```html
Name: Marks:

Grade for {{ student.name }}: {{ grade }}

``` **Flow of control:** 1. User types in `ng-model="student.marks"` → updates `$scope.student.marks` (Model). 2. User clicks button → `computeGrade()` (Controller) is invoked. 3. Controller mutates `$scope.grade` (Model). 4. Digest cycle detects change → `{{ grade }}` in View updates automatically. *(Mark allocation: 3 for diagram/explanation, 2 for Model, 2 for View/Controller code.)* --- #### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):** **Directives** are markers on DOM elements (attributes, element names, comments, or CSS classes) that tell AngularJS's HTML compiler (`$compile`) to attach a specified behavior to that DOM element or transform it. **Six Built-in Directives:** | # | Directive | Purpose | Example | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | `ng-app` | Bootstraps the AngularJS app | `
` | | 2 | `ng-controller` | Attaches a controller to the view | `
` | | 3 | `ng-model` | Two-way binding to a `$scope` property | `` | | 4 | `ng-repeat` | Iterates over a collection | `
  • ` | | 5 | `ng-if` | Conditionally renders an element | `

    Hi!

    ` | | 6 | `ng-click` | Binds a click event to a method | `
    • {{ u.name | uppercase }}
    • ``` **Key methods of `$http`:** `$http.get()`, `$http.post(url, data)`, `$http.put()`, `$http.delete()` — all return a **promise** with `.then(success, error)`. *(Mark allocation: 2 each for service, factory, filter; 1 for $http demonstration.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Confusing AngularJS (1.x) with Angular (2+):** Do not use `TypeScript`, `@Component`, or `[]` bracket syntax. KTU 2024 scheme specifies **AngularJS** — use `$scope`, `ng-app`, `.controller()`, `.service()`. > 2. **Forgetting the array syntax in DI:** Writing `function($scope, $http)` without the array wrapper loses **1–2 marks** even if the logic is correct. > 3. **Not explaining the digest cycle:** A code-only answer for "two-way binding" is incomplete. Always mention **digest cycle** to earn full marks. > 4. **Confusing `ng-if` with `ng-show`:** `ng-if` **removes** the element from the DOM; `ng-show` only toggles CSS. This distinction is a frequent KTU question. > 5. **Missing module injection:** If you write `app.controller(...)`, you must have already declared `var app = angular.module(...)`. Many students forget the dependency array `[]` in module creation. > 6. **Forgetting `ng-app`:** Without `ng-app`, AngularJS never bootstraps — the code "looks right" but does nothing. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **SPA** = one HTML page, dynamic updates via AJAX, no full reloads. - **AngularJS** is a JavaScript MVC/MVW framework by Google (1.x branch). - **`ng-app`** bootstraps the app; **`ng-controller`** attaches business logic. - **Two-way binding** uses **`ng-model`** and is powered by the **digest cycle**. - **`$scope`** is the Model — properties here are bound to the View. - **Directives** extend HTML: `ng-repeat`, `ng-if`, `ng-show`, `ng-click`, `ng-class`, `ng-disabled`. - **Filters** transform data: `{{ value | filterName:arg }}`. - **Services** are singletons for shared state; **Factories** return custom values. - **`$http`** makes AJAX calls and returns **promises** (`.then(success, error)`). - **Dependency Injection** is by parameter name; use **array syntax** for minification safety. - **SPA advantages:** speed, UX, scalability of front-end/back-end separation. - **SPA disadvantages:** SEO challenges, initial load time, requires JavaScript enabled. - **MVC Mapping:** Model = `$scope`, View = HTML template, Controller = `.controller()` function. - **Digest cycle limit:** 10 iterations max — exceeding it throws an error. - **Common built-in services to remember:** `$http`, `$location`, `$timeout`, `$interval`, `$log`, `$rootScope`. --- **End of Module 4 — SPA: Basics, AngularJS Notes**

topics.title: Working with Databases: Databases and Web Development, SQL, Database APIs, Accessing MySQL in PHP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECST832 - WEB PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Working with Databases: Databases and Web Development, SQL, Database APIs, Accessing MySQL in PHP # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Databases in Web Development — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):** > A **Web Database** is a database management system (DBMS) that is exposed to the web through a web server, allowing dynamic, data-driven web applications to **Create, Read, Update, and Delete (CRUD)** persistent information across HTTP requests. In KTU terminology, web databases form the **persistence layer** of the standard 3-tier web architecture: *Presentation Tier (HTML/CSS/JS) → Logic Tier (PHP/Python) → Data Tier (MySQL/PostgreSQL)*. The interaction follows the **LAMP stack paradigm**: - **L**inux — Operating System - **A**pache — Web Server - **M**ySQL — Database - **P**HP — Server-side Scripting > [!NOTE] > **Why Databases in Web Development?** > HTTP is a **stateless protocol** — every request is independent. Without a database, user logins, carts, posts, and search results would vanish after each page reload. Databases provide the **stateful memory** of the web. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen" Think of a web application as a **restaurant**: | Web Component | Restaurant Analogy | Role | |---|---|---| | Web Browser (Client) | Customer | Places order (request) | | Web Server (Apache) | Waiter | Takes order, delivers food | | PHP Script | Chef | Prepares the dish (logic) | | MySQL Database | Cold Storage / Pantry | Stores all ingredients (raw data) | | SQL Query | Recipe Card | Tells the chef how to fetch ingredients | The customer never enters the pantry directly. The waiter (server) takes the request to the chef (PHP), who fetches ingredients from storage (MySQL) using a recipe (SQL query), cooks the dish, and returns it. **PHP is the only language allowed inside the kitchen to talk to the pantry.** ## 1.3 SQL — Structured Query Language > [!IMPORTANT] > **SQL (Structured Query Language)** is the standard **declarative, set-based, fourth-generation language (4GL)** used to define, manipulate, retrieve, and control access to relational databases. It is the **lingua franca** of RDBMS as defined by ISO/IEC 9075. ## 1.4 Database APIs in PHP > [!IMPORTANT] > **Database API (Application Programming Interface)** in PHP is a set of built-in functions or extensions that allow PHP scripts to **connect to, query, and retrieve data from** a database server. KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes **three PHP Database APIs**: | API | Era | Status | Extension | |---|---|---|---| | `mysql_*` functions | PHP 2–5.x | **Removed in PHP 7** | None (procedural) | | `mysqli_*` (Improved) | PHP 5+ | **Active (Recommended)** | Procedural + OOP | | **PDO (PHP Data Objects)** | PHP 5.1+ | **Active (Most Recommended)** | OOP, DB-agnostic | ## 1.5 Accessing MySQL in PHP — The Big Picture > [!NOTE] > Accessing MySQL in PHP is a **5-step ritual** that you must memorize: > 1. **Connect** → `mysqli_connect()` / `new mysqli()` > 2. **Select / Use DB** → `mysqli_select_db()` / `mysqli_db_select()` > 3. **Query** → `mysqli_query()` / `$db->query()` > 4. **Fetch Result** → `mysqli_fetch_array()` / `fetch_assoc()` > 5. **Close** → `mysqli_close()` / `$db->close()` ## 1.6 Important Metrics & Standards - **Default MySQL Port:** **3306** - **Default MySQL User:** `root` - **Default Web Port:** **80** (HTTP), **443** (HTTPS) - **SQL Standards:** ISO/IEC 9075:2023 - **MySQL Version Targeted by KTU:** **MySQL 5.7+ / 8.0** - **PHP Version Targeted:** **PHP 7.x / 8.x** # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 SQL Command Taxonomy SQL is divided into **five sublanguages** (high-yield for KTU exams): ### (a) DDL — Data Definition Language Defines the **schema** (structure). | Command | Purpose | Example | |---|---|---| | `CREATE` | Make new table/DB | `CREATE TABLE Student (id INT, name VARCHAR(50));` | | `ALTER` | Modify structure | `ALTER TABLE Student ADD age INT;` | | `DROP` | Delete permanently | `DROP TABLE Student;` | | `TRUNCATE` | Empty table (faster than DELETE) | `TRUNCATE TABLE Student;` | | `RENAME` | Rename object | `RENAME TABLE Student TO Learner;` | ### (b) DML — Data Manipulation Language Manipulates the **data** (rows). | Command | Purpose | Example | |---|---|---| | `INSERT` | Add new rows | `INSERT INTO Student VALUES (1, 'Anu');` | | `UPDATE` | Modify existing rows | `UPDATE Student SET name='Anu S' WHERE id=1;` | | `DELETE` | Remove rows | `DELETE FROM Student WHERE id=1;` | ### (c) DQL — Data Query Language **Retrieves** data. | Command | Purpose | Example | |---|---|---| | `SELECT` | Fetch data | `SELECT * FROM Student WHERE age > 18;` | ### (d) DCL — Data Control Language Controls **permissions**. | Command | Purpose | Example | |---|---|---| | `GRANT` | Give privileges | `GRANT ALL ON db.* TO 'user'@'localhost';` | | `REVOKE` | Remove privileges | `REVOKE ALL ON db.* FROM 'user'@'localhost';` | ### (e) TCL — Transaction Control Language Manages **transactions** (atomicity). | Command | Purpose | Example | |---|---|---| | `COMMIT` | Save changes permanently | `COMMIT;` | | `ROLLBACK` | Undo uncommitted changes | `ROLLBACK;` | | `SAVEPOINT` | Mark rollback point | `SAVEPOINT sp1;` | ## 2.2 SQL Data Types (High-Yield Table) | Category | MySQL Type | Use Case | Storage | |---|---|---|---| | Integer | `TINYINT`, `INT`, `BIGINT` | IDs, counts | 1, 4, 8 bytes | | Decimal | `DECIMAL(M,D)`, `FLOAT` | Money, scientific | Variable | | String | `CHAR(n)`, `VARCHAR(n)` | Fixed/variable text | n bytes / L+1 | | Text | `TEXT`, `MEDIUMTEXT`, `LONGTEXT` | Long content | 64KB – 4GB | | Date/Time | `DATE`, `DATETIME`, `TIMESTAMP` | Timestamps | 3–8 bytes | | Boolean | `BOOLEAN` (alias for `TINYINT(1)`) | Flags | 1 byte | ## 2.3 SQL Constraints > [!IMPORTANT] > **Constraints** are rules enforced on data columns to maintain **data integrity** — a very common KTU 2-mark question. | Constraint | Meaning | Example | |---|---|---| | `PRIMARY KEY` | Unique + NOT NULL | `id INT PRIMARY KEY` | | `FOREIGN KEY` | Referential integrity | `FOREIGN KEY (dept_id) REFERENCES Dept(id)` | | `UNIQUE` | No duplicate values | `email VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE` | | `NOT NULL` | Cannot be empty | `name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL` | | `DEFAULT` | Default value | `status BOOLEAN DEFAULT 1` | | `CHECK` | Custom condition | `CHECK (age >= 18)` | | `AUTO_INCREMENT` | Auto-generated sequence | `id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY` | ## 2.4 The SELECT Statement — Anatomy ```text SELECT [DISTINCT] column_list FROM table_name [WHERE condition] [GROUP BY col] [HAVING group_condition] [ORDER BY col [ASC|DESC]] [LIMIT n OFFSET m]; ``` > [!NOTE] > **Logical Execution Order (NOT writing order):** > `FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → DISTINCT → ORDER BY → LIMIT` > This is a **favourite KTU question** — students often confuse WHERE and HAVING. ## 2.5 SQL JOINs — The Most Tested Topic > [!IMPORTANT] > **JOINs combine rows from two or more tables** based on a related column. KTU almost always tests this with 7–14 mark questions. | JOIN Type | Returns | |---|---| | `INNER JOIN` | Only matching rows in both tables | | `LEFT JOIN` | All left rows + matched right (NULL if no match) | | `RIGHT JOIN` | All right rows + matched left (NULL if no match) | | `FULL OUTER JOIN` | All rows from both (NULL where no match) — *not in MySQL by default* | | `CROSS JOIN` | Cartesian product | | `SELF JOIN` | Table joined with itself | ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is a **cheat sheet** of all critical PHP-MySQL constructs you must memorize. Note the use of `\vert` for separators to avoid breaking table syntax. | Concept | Syntax / Formula | Notes | |---|---|---| | Connect (procedural) | `$conn = mysqli_connect(host, user, pass, db);` | Returns object or `false` | | Connect (OOP) | `$conn = new mysqli(host, user, pass, db);` | Throws on error | | Check connection | `if (!$conn) { die(mysqli_connect_error()); }` | Always test | | Select DB | `mysqli_select_db($conn, "mydb");` | Skip if passed in connect | | Run query | `$result = mysqli_query($conn, $sql);` | For `SELECT` returns result set | | Fetch row (assoc) | `$row = mysqli_fetch_assoc($result);` | Returns associative array | | Fetch row (array) | `$row = mysqli_fetch_array($result, MYSQLI_BOTH);` | Numeric + assoc keys | | Fetch all | `$rows = mysqli_fetch_all($result, MYSQLI_ASSOC);` | PHP 5.3+ | | Num rows | `$n = mysqli_num_rows($result);` | Only for SELECT | | Affected rows | `$n = mysqli_affected_rows($conn);` | For INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE | | Escape string | `$safe = mysqli_real_escape_string($conn, $input);` | Prevents **SQL Injection** | | Prepared stmt | `$stmt = $conn->prepare("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?");` | **Gold standard** security | | Bind params | `$stmt->bind_param("i", $id);` | i=integer, s=string, d=double, b=blob | | Execute | `$stmt->execute();` | Returns boolean | | Get result | `$res = $stmt->get_result();` | Then `fetch_assoc()` | | Close stmt | `$stmt->close();` | Free resources | | Close conn | `mysqli_close($conn);` or `$conn->close();` | Always close | | Default port | **3306** | `localhost:3306` | | Query error | `die(mysqli_error($conn));` | Debug helper | | Character set | `mysqli_set_charset($conn, "utf8");` | Prevents garbled text | | Aggregation | `COUNT(*), SUM(col), AVG(col), MIN(col), MAX(col)` | Used with `GROUP BY` | ## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering Database-driven web development is the backbone of: - **E-Commerce:** Amazon, Flipkart — product catalogs, user carts, orders. - **Social Media:** Facebook, Instagram — user profiles, posts, friend graphs. - **Banking:** Transaction records, audit trails. - **Healthcare:** Patient records, prescriptions (HIPAA-compliant). - **IoT Dashboards:** Sensor data stored in MySQL, displayed via PHP dashboards. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** When a B.Tech student builds a final-year project, **80% of project marks** in KTU valuation come from the **database integration logic**, not just the UI. Mastering this module is non-negotiable. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 The Complete PHP–MySQL Connectivity Workflow (Procedural Style) Below is the **exhaustive, KTU-exact** step-by-step derivation of a typical PHP-MySQL interaction. ### Step 1 — Database Creation (Run in MySQL CLI / phpMyAdmin) ```sql CREATE DATABASE IF NOT EXISTS ktuwebdb; USE ktuwebdb; CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS students ( id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY, name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, email VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE, branch VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'CSE', cgpa DECIMAL(4,2) CHECK (cgpa BETWEEN 0 AND 10), created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP ); ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why this order?** Database must exist *before* PHP tries to connect to it. Table must exist *before* any CRUD operation. This mirrors the **dependency hierarchy** of relational design. ### Step 2 — PHP Connection Script (`db_connect.php`) ```php ``` **Line-by-line reasoning:** - *Line A:* Credentials are usually stored in a **separate config file** to follow separation of concerns. - *Line B:* `mysqli_connect()` returns a connection handle (object) on success or `false` on failure. - *Line C:* The `die()` function halts execution and prints the message — useful in dev, **dangerous in production** (info leak). - *Line D:* UTF-8 prevents issues with Indian names containing Malayalam/Hindi characters. ### Step 3 — INSERT Operation (`insert.php`) ```php ``` > [!WARNING] > **SQL Injection Vulnerability:** The above code is **dangerous**. A malicious user can input `' OR '1'='1` to drop or dump your entire database. KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly tests whether you know how to prevent this via **prepared statements**. ### Step 4 — Secure INSERT with Prepared Statements (Gold Standard) ```php prepare("INSERT INTO students (name, email, branch, cgpa) VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?)"); // Step H: Bind parameters ("sssd" = 3 strings + 1 double) $stmt->bind_param("sssd", $name, $email, $branch, $cgpa); // Step I: Execute if ($stmt->execute()) { echo "Inserted securely. ID: " . $stmt->insert_id; } else { echo "Error: " . $stmt->error; } $stmt->close(); mysqli_close($conn); ?> ``` **Why prepared statements are secure:** 1. SQL query structure is sent to MySQL **first, with placeholders**. 2. User input is sent **separately** as bound parameters. 3. MySQL treats the input **purely as data**, never as executable SQL. 4. Even input like `'; DROP TABLE students; --` is stored as a literal string. ### Step 5 — SELECT and Display (`view.php`) ```php ? ORDER BY cgpa DESC"; $stmt = $conn->prepare($sql); $threshold = 7.5; $stmt->bind_param("d", $threshold); $stmt->execute(); $result = $stmt->get_result(); echo ""; while ($row = $result->fetch_assoc()) { echo ""; echo ""; echo ""; echo ""; echo ""; echo ""; echo ""; } echo "
IDNameEmailBranchCGPA
" . htmlspecialchars($row['id']) . "" . htmlspecialchars($row['name']) . "" . htmlspecialchars($row['email']) . "" . htmlspecialchars($row['branch']) . "" . htmlspecialchars($row['cgpa']) . "
"; $stmt->close(); mysqli_close($conn); ?> ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why `htmlspecialchars()`?** Even after a secure SQL query, the **output** could contain malicious HTML/JS (XSS attack). Always escape output. KTU examiners check this in 14-mark questions. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right API | Criterion | `mysql_*` (legacy) | `mysqli` | **PDO** | |---|---|---|---| | Available in PHP 7+ | ❌ Removed | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | | Procedural support | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | | OOP support | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | | Prepared statements | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | | Multiple DB support | ❌ MySQL only | ❌ MySQL only | ✅ 12+ drivers | | Named parameters | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ `:name` | | Exception handling | ❌ | Manual | ✅ Try-catch | | KTU 2024 Recommendation | **Avoid** | **Acceptable** | **Preferred** | ## 3.3 The Authentication Logic Derivation (Full Login System) A complete KTU-level PHP-MySQL login example: ```php prepare($sql); $stmt->bind_param("s", $username); $stmt->execute(); $result = $stmt->get_result(); if ($result->num_rows === 1) { $row = $result->fetch_assoc(); if (password_verify($password, $row['password_hash'])) { $_SESSION['user_id'] = $row['id']; header("Location: dashboard.php"); exit(); } else { echo "Invalid password."; } } else { echo "User not found."; } $stmt->close(); mysqli_close($conn); ?> ``` **Step-by-step logic:** 1. `session_start()` begins a server-side session. 2. User credentials posted from an HTML form. 3. SQL fetches the **hash** of the password (not the password itself). 4. `password_verify()` uses bcrypt to compare plaintext input with stored hash. 5. On success, a session variable is set and user is redirected. 6. On failure, a generic error is shown (do not reveal whether username exists — security best practice). ## 3.4 PDO Alternative (DB-Agnostic) ```php setAttribute(PDO::ATTR_ERRMODE, PDO::ERRMODE_EXCEPTION); $stmt = $pdo->prepare("SELECT * FROM students WHERE branch = :branch"); $stmt->execute([':branch' => 'CSE']); foreach ($stmt->fetchAll(PDO::FETCH_ASSOC) as $row) { echo $row['name'] . "
"; } } catch (PDOException $e) { echo "Error: " . $e->getMessage(); } ?> ``` > [!NOTE] > **PDO advantage:** To switch from MySQL to PostgreSQL, you only change the **DSN string** (`mysql:` → `pgsql:`). The rest of the code remains identical. This is the **"write once, deploy anywhere"** philosophy. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 3-Tier Web-Database Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB client1[Web Browser Client] server1[Apache Web Server] logic1[PHP Engine Logic Tier] data1[MySQL Database Data Tier] client1 -->|HTTP Request HTML CSS JS| server1 server1 -->|Forward .php Request| logic1 logic1 -->|SQL Query via mysqli PDO| data1 data1 -->|Result Set Rows| logic1 logic1 -->|Rendered HTML| server1 server1 -->|HTTP Response| client1 ``` ## 4.2 PHP–MySQL Connectivity Flow (Sequential) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Step 1 Define Credentials host user pass db] --> B[Step 2 mysqli_connect OR new mysqli] B --> C{Step 3 Connection Successful?} C -- No --> D[die mysqli_connect_error Exit] C -- Yes --> E[Step 4 mysqli_set_charset utf8] E --> F[Step 5 Build SQL String] F --> G[Step 6 Prepare Statement Recommended] G --> H[Step 7 bind_param ssid etc] H --> I[Step 8 Execute Query] I --> J{Query Type} J -- SELECT --> K[Step 9a get_result then fetch_assoc Loop] J -- INSERT UPDATE DELETE --> L[Step 9b Check affected_rows or insert_id] K --> M[Step 10 stmt close then mysqli close] L --> M ``` ## 4.3 SQL JOIN Conceptual Map ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SQ[JOIN Types in SQL] direction TB J1[INNER JOIN - Only Matching Rows] J2[LEFT JOIN - All Left Plus Matches] J3[RIGHT JOIN - All Right Plus Matches] J4[FULL OUTER JOIN - All Rows Both Sides] J5[CROSS JOIN - Cartesian Product N x M] J6[SELF JOIN - Table Joined With Itself] end ``` ## 4.4 Database API Evolution Timeline ```mermaid flowchart LR T1[PHP 2 1997 mysql extension] --> T2[PHP 5 2004 mysqli procedural plus OOP] T2 --> T3[PHP 5.1 2005 PDO introduced] T3 --> T4[PHP 7 2015 mysql removed] T4 --> T5[PHP 8.x Current mysqli and PDO only] ``` ## 4.5 SQL Command Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TB SQL[SQL Structured Query Language] SQL --> DDL[DDL - Data Definition] SQL --> DML[DML - Data Manipulation] SQL --> DQL[DQL - Data Query] SQL --> DCL[DCL - Data Control] SQL --> TCL[TCL - Transaction Control] DDL --> D1[CREATE] DDL --> D2[ALTER] DDL --> D3[DROP] DDL --> D4[TRUNCATE] DML --> M1[INSERT] DML --> M2[UPDATE] DML --> M3[DELETE] DQL --> Q1[SELECT] DCL --> C1[GRANT] DCL --> C2[REVOKE] TCL --> TC1[COMMIT] TCL --> TC2[ROLLBACK] TCL --> TC3[SAVEPOINT] ``` ## 4.6 CRUD-to-HTTP Mapping | CRUD Operation | SQL Command | HTTP Method | PHP Function | |---|---|---|---| | **C**reate | `INSERT` | `POST` | `mysqli_query` with INSERT | | **R**ead | `SELECT` | `GET` | `mysqli_query` + `fetch_assoc` | | **U**pdate | `UPDATE` | `PUT / PATCH` | `mysqli_query` with UPDATE | | **D**elete | `DELETE` | `DELETE` | `mysqli_query` with DELETE | # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Differentiate between DDL and DML commands in SQL. Give two examples for each.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | DDL (Data Definition Language) | DML (Data Manipulation Language) | |---|---|---| | Purpose | Defines or alters the **structure** (schema) of database objects | Manipulates the **data** (rows) inside tables | | Auto-commit | Yes (changes are permanent immediately) | No (can be rolled back) | | Affects | Table structure, indexes, views | Table data only | | Example 1 | `CREATE TABLE Student (id INT, name VARCHAR(50));` | `INSERT INTO Student VALUES (1, 'Anu');` | | Example 2 | `ALTER TABLE Student ADD age INT;` | `UPDATE Student SET age = 21 WHERE id = 1;` | | Example 3 | `DROP TABLE Student;` | `DELETE FROM Student WHERE id = 1;` | > **Mark Split:** (1 mark) Definition of DDL + (1 mark) Definition of DML + (1 mark) Two examples each. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Explain the role of the `mysqli` extension in PHP. Why is it preferred over the older `mysql_*` functions?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** `mysqli` (MySQL Improved) is the modern PHP extension for interacting with MySQL databases. It is preferred over the legacy `mysql_*` functions for the following reasons: 1. **Active Support:** `mysql_*` functions were **removed in PHP 7.0** and produce fatal errors in any modern PHP environment. `mysqli` is the officially supported replacement. 2. **Security:** `mysqli` natively supports **prepared statements** with parameter binding, which prevents **SQL Injection** attacks — the legacy functions had no such feature. 3. **Dual Paradigm:** `mysqli` supports both **procedural** (`mysqli_query($conn, $sql)`) and **object-oriented** (`$conn->query($sql)`) styles, giving developers flexibility. 4. **Enhanced Features:** Supports **transactions, multi-query execution, stored procedures, and asynchronous queries** — none available in the old API. > **Mark Split:** (1 mark) Role/definition + (1 mark) Two reasons + (1 mark) Example or extra point. --- ## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts. The student answers **one full question** (either Or choice). Each sub-part is typically 7 marks. --- ### **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified]** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Design a MySQL database schema for an online bookstore with tables `Books`, `Authors`, and `Customers`. Include appropriate primary keys, foreign keys, and constraints. Write the SQL DDL for the same.** **Model Solution:** ```sql -- 1. Authors Table CREATE TABLE Authors ( author_id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY, author_name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL, country VARCHAR(50) DEFAULT 'India' ); -- 2. Books Table CREATE TABLE Books ( book_id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY, title VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL, price DECIMAL(8,2) NOT NULL CHECK (price > 0), stock INT DEFAULT 0, author_id INT NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY (author_id) REFERENCES Authors(author_id) ON DELETE CASCADE ON UPDATE CASCADE ); -- 3. Customers Table CREATE TABLE Customers ( cust_id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY, cust_name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL, email VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE NOT NULL, phone VARCHAR(15), reg_date DATE DEFAULT (CURRENT_DATE) ); ``` **Explanation (Valuation Key):** - `AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY` on every table → 1 mark each (3 tables). - `FOREIGN KEY` on Books referencing Authors → 2 marks. - `CHECK`, `UNIQUE`, `NOT NULL`, `DEFAULT` constraints → 1 mark. - Correct syntax and cascade rules → 1 mark. --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Write a PHP script using `mysqli` prepared statements to (i) insert a new book and (ii) fetch and display all books priced above ₹500. Show proper connection handling and closing.** **Model Solution:** ```php connect_error) { die("Connection failed: " . $conn->connect_error); } $conn->set_charset("utf8"); // (i) INSERT a new book $title = "Web Programming with PHP"; $price = 650.00; $stock = 25; $author_id = 1; $stmt = $conn->prepare("INSERT INTO Books (title, price, stock, author_id) VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?)"); $stmt->bind_param("sdii", $title, $price, $stock, $author_id); if ($stmt->execute()) { echo "Book inserted with ID: " . $stmt->insert_id . "
"; } else { echo "Insert error: " . $stmt->error; } $stmt->close(); // (ii) FETCH books priced above 500 $threshold = 500.00; $stmt2 = $conn->prepare("SELECT book_id, title, price FROM Books WHERE price > ? ORDER BY price DESC"); $stmt2->bind_param("d", $threshold); $stmt2->execute(); $result = $stmt2->get_result(); echo ""; while ($row = $result->fetch_assoc()) { echo ""; } echo "
IDTitlePrice
{$row['book_id']}{$row['title']}{$row['price']}
"; $stmt2->close(); $conn->close(); ?> ``` **Mark Distribution (Valuation Key):** - Connection setup + charset → 1 mark - Prepared statement for INSERT + bind_param → 2 marks - Prepared statement for SELECT + execution → 2 marks - Fetch loop + HTML table rendering → 1.5 marks - Proper close() calls → 0.5 mark --- ### **Q4. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified] — OR Alternative** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Explain the different types of JOINs in SQL with suitable examples. Write a query to display customer names and the total amount they have spent using an `Orders` table.** **Model Solution:** **Types of JOINs:** 1. **INNER JOIN:** Returns rows where the join condition matches in *both* tables. ```sql SELECT c.name, o.amount FROM Customers c INNER JOIN Orders o ON c.cust_id = o.cust_id; ``` 2. **LEFT (OUTER) JOIN:** Returns *all* rows from the left table, and matched rows from the right (NULL if no match). ```sql SELECT c.name, o.amount FROM Customers c LEFT JOIN Orders o ON c.cust_id = o.cust_id; ``` 3. **RIGHT (OUTER) JOIN:** Mirror of LEFT — all rows from the right table. 4. **FULL OUTER JOIN:** All rows from both. *(Not natively supported in MySQL — emulate with `UNION` of LEFT and RIGHT.)* 5. **CROSS JOIN:** Cartesian product — every row in A paired with every row in B. 6. **SELF JOIN:** A table joined with itself — used for hierarchical data (e.g., employee-manager). **Query for total amount per customer:** ```sql SELECT c.cust_name, SUM(o.amount) AS total_spent FROM Customers c INNER JOIN Orders o ON c.cust_id = o.cust_id GROUP BY c.cust_id, c.cust_name HAVING total_spent > 1000 ORDER BY total_spent DESC; ``` **Mark Split:** (3 marks) JOIN types explanation + (2 marks) Example queries + (2 marks) Aggregation query with GROUP BY / HAVING. --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Discuss SQL Injection attacks. Show how prepared statements in PHP prevent them. Provide a vulnerable code snippet and its secure counterpart.** **Model Solution:** **SQL Injection** is a code-injection technique where an attacker inserts malicious SQL into a query string, manipulating the query's logic. Example attack input for a login form: ``` Username: admin' -- Password: anything ``` **Vulnerable Code (Do NOT use in production):** ```php ``` **Attacker's input** transforms the query to: ```sql SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = 'admin' -- ' AND password = 'anything' ``` The `--` comments out the password check, granting unauthorized access. **Secure Code (Prepared Statement):** ```php prepare("SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = ? AND password_hash = ?"); $stmt->bind_param("ss", $user, $pass); $stmt->execute(); $result = $stmt->get_result(); ?> ``` **Why it's secure:** - The query structure (`SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = ? AND password_hash = ?`) is sent to MySQL **first** and compiled. - The user input is sent **separately** and bound by `bind_param`. - MySQL treats input as **pure data**, never as executable SQL. - Even if the input is `' OR '1'='1`, it is stored as a literal string and matched against `username` — no match found, no breach. **Additional defenses:** Use `password_hash()` + `password_verify()` for passwords, `htmlspecialchars()` for output, and enable error logging (not display) in production. **Mark Split:** (2 marks) Definition of SQLi + (2 marks) Vulnerable example + (3 marks) Prepared statement explanation + secured code. --- > [!WARNING] > **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting `bind_param` type string** (e.g., `"ssi"` vs `"sss"`) — loses 1–2 marks. > 2. **Not closing statements / connections** — loses 0.5–1 mark. > 3. **Writing raw `$_POST` values directly into SQL** — even in "learning" examples, examiners may deduct for not mentioning the word *"prepared statement"*. > 4. **Confusing `mysqli_num_rows`** (for SELECT) with `mysqli_affected_rows` (for INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE) — a classic trap. > 5. **Skipping `die()` / error check on `mysqli_connect()`** — examiner expects defensive code. > 6. **Writing JOIN queries without specifying the join condition** (e.g., `FROM A JOIN B`) — loses 1 mark. > 7. **Forgetting `GROUP BY` when using aggregate functions** — the query will throw an error in strict mode. > 8. **Using the wrong slash direction in PHP tags or HTML form actions** — silly but costly. --- ## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize This Before the Exam:** - **Database in Web:** A persistent storage layer exposed via the web to overcome HTTP's statelessness. Forms the **3rd tier** in the 3-tier architecture. - **MySQL Default Port:** **3306** | Root user default with no password (XAMPP/WAMP). - **SQL Sublanguages:** **DDL, DML, DQL, DCL, TCL** — know at least 2 commands from each. - **CRUD ↔ SQL ↔ HTTP:** Create→INSERT→POST, Read→SELECT→GET, Update→UPDATE→PUT, Delete→DELETE→DELETE. - **Primary Key:** Unique + NOT NULL. One per table. **Foreign Key:** Refers to a primary key in another (or same) table. - **WHERE vs HAVING:** `WHERE` filters rows *before* grouping; `HAVING` filters groups *after* aggregation. - **JOIN Types:** `INNER` (intersection), `LEFT` (all left), `RIGHT` (all right), `FULL`, `CROSS`, `SELF`. - **ORDER OF EXECUTION:** `FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → ORDER BY → LIMIT`. - **PHP APIs:** ❌ `mysql_*` (dead) | ✅ `mysqli` | ⭐ **PDO (best, DB-agnostic)**. - **5-Step PHP-MySQL Ritual:** `connect → select db → query → fetch → close`. - **Prepared Statement Workflow:** `prepare() → bind_param() → execute() → get_result() → fetch_*() → close()`. - **bind_param type codes:** `s`=string, `i`=integer, `d`=double, `b`=blob. - **SQL Injection Prevention:** Always use **prepared statements** + `bind_param`. Never interpolate user input into SQL strings. - **XSS Prevention:** Use `htmlspecialchars()` on all output. - **Password Storage:** Use `password_hash()` (bcrypt) + `password_verify()` — never `MD5` or plain text. - **Error Handling:** `die(mysqli_error($conn))` in development; log to file in production. - **Connection Close:** Always call `mysqli_close()` / `$conn->close()` to free resources. - **Charset:** Always `mysqli_set_charset($conn, "utf8")` to support Indian languages. - **Result Set Functions:** `fetch_assoc()` (assoc), `fetch_row()` (numeric), `fetch_array()` (both), `fetch_all()` (all rows). - **Row Count:** `mysqli_num_rows($result)` for SELECT | `mysqli_affected_rows($conn)` for INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE. - **Transactions:** Wrap multi-step DB operations in `BEGIN; ... COMMIT;` or `ROLLBACK;` for atomicity. - **PDO DSN Examples:** `mysql:host=localhost;dbname=mydb` | `pgsql:host=...` | `sqlite:path/to/file.db`. - **KTU's Favourite Questions:** JOIN queries, GROUP BY + HAVING, prepared statements, schema design with FK, full login example. > **🎯 Final Tip:** In KTU 14-mark answers, always include **(1) the conceptual explanation, (2) the syntax/code, (3) a small real-world example, and (4) a security/best-practice note.** Examiners reward structure and completeness over brevity.

topics.title: Web Application Design: Real World Web Software Design, Principle of Layering, Software Design Patterns in the Web Context, Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST832 - WEB PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Web Application Design: Real World Web Software Design, Principle of Layering, Software Design Patterns in the Web Context, Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Real World Web Software Design — The KTU 2024 Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> *Real World Web Software Design* is the structured, disciplined process of translating business requirements into a maintainable, scalable, and testable web application architecture. It encompasses requirement gathering, architectural decisions, technology stack selection, and the systematic decomposition of the system into well-defined modules.

In KTU's Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, web software design is treated as the bridge between **system analysis** and **implementation**. The deliverable of this phase is a *Design Document* (the DD or the *Software Design Specification — SDS*) that guides every subsequent coding decision.

### Conceptual Analogy — "Building a House"

Imagine you are going to build a house (your web application). Before laying a single brick, you need:

| House Construction Phase | Web Software Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Talking to the family about needs | **Requirement Analysis** (User Stories, Use Cases) |
| Hiring an architect for the blueprint | **High-Level Design (HLD)** — Architecture, Layering |
| Detailed floor plan with measurements | **Low-Level Design (LLD)** — Classes, APIs, DB Schema |
| Electrical and plumbing layout | **Cross-Cutting Concerns** — Security, Logging, Caching |
| Inspection of the finished house | **Testing** — Unit, Integration, System, UAT |

A *real-world* web software design acknowledges that the application will live for **5–10 years**, will be maintained by **multiple teams**, must handle **10× traffic spikes**, and must survive **security attacks**.

### 1.1.1 Key Quality Attributes of a Well-Designed Web App

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A KTU examiner expects you to know these *-ility* attributes verbatim.

1. **Scalability** — The system handles growing load by adding resources (horizontal or vertical).
2. **Maintainability** — New developers can understand and modify code within weeks.
3. **Reusability** — Components are built once and reused across the application.
4. **Testability** — Each unit can be verified in isolation.
5. **Security** — Authentication, authorization, and data protection are baked in.
6. **Performance** — Page load under **2 seconds** on a 3G connection.
7. **Usability** — Intuitive UI, accessible to WCAG 2.1 standards.
8. **Portability** — Runs on multiple browsers, OSes, and devices.

---

## 1.2 Principle of Layering — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):**
> *Layering* (or *Tiered Architecture*) is the architectural principle of organizing a web application into a stack of **horizontally separated**, **logically distinct** layers, where each layer has a specific responsibility and communicates *only* with the layer immediately below it.

The *Seven-Layer OSI* model of networking is the canonical inspiration. In web software, the most common layering is the **3-Tier** or **N-Tier** model.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen"

Picture a fine-dining restaurant:

- **Customers** place orders → they only talk to the *Waiter*.
- **Waiter** takes the order → talks to the *Chef*.
- **Chef** prepares the dish → uses ingredients from the *Cold Storage*.
- **Cold Storage** doesn't know about customers. **Chef** doesn't know who the customer is.

Each role has **one** job. If the chef is swapped, the waiter still works. This is *Layering*.

> [!TIP]
> If a layer in your design knows *too much* about another layer, you have **tight coupling** — a KTU examiner's red flag.

---

## 1.3 Software Design Patterns in the Web Context

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A *Design Pattern* is a **reusable, proven solution** to a recurring problem in software design. It is a *template* — not finished code — that captures the essence of a solution so it can be applied across many contexts (Gamma et al., *Gang of Four*, 1994).

In the web context, patterns are particularly powerful because web apps face a small set of well-understood problems: serving thousands of users, decoupling front-end from back-end, persisting state, handling failures, etc.

### Conceptual Analogy — "Recipes in a Cookbook"

A *recipe* for *dosa* is a **pattern** — you can use it with different flours, fillings, and pans. You don't invent cooking from scratch each morning; you reuse a tested recipe. Design patterns work exactly like this: *MVC* is the recipe for separating concerns; *Singleton* is the recipe for a single shared resource; *Observer* is the recipe for one-to-many notification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three families of patterns every KTU student must know:**
> 1. **Creational** — How objects are *created* (Singleton, Factory, Builder, Prototype).
> 2. **Structural** — How classes/objects are *composed* (Adapter, Decorator, Facade, Proxy).
> 3. **Behavioral** — How objects *communicate* (Observer, Strategy, MVC, Iterator, Command).

---

## 1.4 Testing — The Quality Gate

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 / IEEE 829):**
> *Software Testing* is the empirical, technical investigation conducted against a web application to provide stakeholders with information about its quality, correctness, completeness, and risk. Testing is the *process of executing a program with the intent of finding defects* (Glenford Myers, 1979).

### Conceptual Analogy — "Quality Check at the Bakery"

Before bread reaches the customer, it is checked for:
- **Taste** (Functional test)
- **Weight & Size** (Performance test)
- **No stones/burns** (Defect test)
- **Survives transport** (Stress test)
- **Listed ingredients match packet** (Compliance test)

Web testing follows the same philosophy.

> [!TIP]
> KTU expects you to know the **V-Model** of testing, where each development phase has a corresponding test phase.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Real World Web Software Design — Detailed Workflow

A real-world web project follows a *phased* lifecycle. The exact phases are non-negotiable in industry and in the KTU syllabus.

### 2.1.1 The 7-Phase Web Design Workflow

1. **Requirement Elicitation**
   - Functional Requirements (FR): *"User can reset password via email."*
   - Non-Functional Requirements (NFR): *"Password reset email must arrive in < 30 seconds."*
   - Output: **SRS (Software Requirement Specification) document**.

2. **Feasibility Study**
   - **Technical Feasibility** — Can we build it with our stack?
   - **Economic Feasibility** — Is the ROI positive?
   - **Operational Feasibility** — Will users adopt it?
   - **Schedule Feasibility** — Can we deliver in time?
   - **Legal Feasibility** — GDPR, IT Act 2000 (India) compliance.

3. **Architecture Selection** — See Section 2.2.

4. **High-Level Design (HLD)**
   - Module breakdown
   - Database schema (ER diagram)
   - External interfaces (APIs, third-party services)
   - Output: **HLD Document**.

5. **Low-Level Design (LLD)**
   - Class diagrams, sequence diagrams, state diagrams
   - Algorithm pseudocode for critical modules
   - API contracts (REST endpoints, GraphQL schemas)
   - Output: **LLD Document**.

6. **Implementation / Coding** — Developers write code per the LLD.

7. **Testing & Deployment** — See Section 2.5.

---

## 2.2 Principle of Layering — Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 The Classic 3-Tier Architecture

The **3-Tier** model is the de-facto standard for web apps. Each *tier* is a logical — and often physical — separation.

| Tier # | Name | Responsibility | Technology Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tier 1 | **Presentation Layer** | UI, user input rendering, client-side validation | HTML5, CSS3, JavaScript, React, Angular, Vue.js |
| Tier 2 | **Application / Business Logic Layer** | Core business rules, data validation, workflow orchestration | Java (Spring), Python (Django/Flask), Node.js (Express), PHP (Laravel), C# (.NET) |
| Tier 3 | **Data Layer** | Persistent storage, retrieval, transactions | MySQL, PostgreSQL, MongoDB, Oracle, Redis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strict Rule:** The Presentation Layer **NEVER** talks to the Data Layer directly. The Business Layer is the *only* gatekeeper. Violations are called *layer-leakage* and cost marks in KTU exams.

### 2.2.2 The N-Tier (Multi-Tier) Extension

Modern web systems add more layers:

$$
\text{N-Tier} = \text{Presentation} + \text{API Gateway} + \text{Business} + \text{Cache} + \text{Data} + \text{Message Queue}
$$

A typical **e-commerce** N-tier deployment:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{User} \rightarrow \text{CDN} &\rightarrow \text{Load Balancer (Nginx)} \\
&\rightarrow \text{API Gateway (Kong/AWS API GW)} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Auth Service} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Product Service (Microservice)} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Cart Service} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Order Service} \\
&\rightarrow \text{PostgreSQL + Redis Cache + RabbitMQ}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.2.3 Benefits of Layering

- **Separation of Concerns** — Each layer has one job.
- **Independent Deployability** — Replace the front-end framework without touching the back-end.
- **Parallel Development** — Front-end and back-end teams work simultaneously.
- **Easier Testing** — Mock the layer below for unit tests.
- **Security** — Business logic hidden behind a validated API surface.

### 2.2.4 Drawbacks (Be Honest in Exams)

- **Performance Overhead** — Each hop adds latency.
- **Complexity** — More files, more modules, more deployables.
- **Distributed System Bugs** — Network failures, serialization issues.
- **Over-engineering** — A 5-page static site does not need 3 tiers.

---

## 2.3 Design Patterns in the Web Context — The KTU Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table. It is the single most exam-rewarding content in this module.

| Pattern | Category | Web Use Case | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MVC** (Model-View-Controller) | Behavioral (Architectural) | Separates UI from data and logic | Django, Spring MVC, Ruby on Rails, Express |
| **MVP** (Model-View-Presenter) | Behavioral | UI testing with a mockable Presenter | ASP.NET Web Forms (legacy), GWT |
| **MVVM** (Model-View-ViewModel) | Behavioral | Two-way data binding in SPAs | Angular, Vue.js, WPF, Knockout |
| **Singleton** | Creational | DB connection pool, Logger, Config | Java `Runtime.getRuntime()`, Node.js `module.exports` |
| **Factory Method** | Creational | Creating different HTTP clients based on URL | `axios.create()`, Java `Calendar.getInstance()` |
| **Builder** | Creational | Building complex query objects | `StringBuilder`, SQL `QueryBuilder`, Lombok `@Builder` |
| **Prototype** | Creational | Cloning pre-configured components | JavaScript `Object.create()`, `Object.assign()` |
| **Adapter** | Structural | Bridging legacy API to new client | jQuery `.ajax()` adapting to `fetch` |
| **Decorator** | Structural | Adding middleware to requests | Express middleware chain, Python decorators |
| **Facade** | Structural | Simplified API over a complex subsystem | `jQuery` (a facade over raw DOM), `axios` |
| **Proxy** | Structural | Lazy loading, caching, access control | Service Worker, CDN edge proxy, Spring `@Transactional` |
| **Observer** | Behavioral | Pub/Sub for real-time updates | DOM event listeners, Node.js `EventEmitter`, WebSockets |
| **Strategy** | Behavioral | Swappable algorithms at runtime | Payment gateway selection (Stripe / PayPal / Razorpay) |
| **Command** | Behavioral | Encapsulating a request as an object | Undo/Redo, Task queues (Celery, BullMQ) |
| **Iterator** | Behavioral | Sequential traversal of collections | `for...of`, Java `Iterator`, LINQ |
| **Front Controller** | Architectural | Single entry point for all requests | `index.php`, Express `app.use()`, Spring `DispatcherServlet` |
| **DAO** (Data Access Object) | Architectural | Hides DB specifics from business layer | Hibernate DAO, JDBC `PreparedStatement` wrapper |
| **Repository** | Architectural | Collection-like access to data | Spring Data `JpaRepository`, TypeORM |

---

## 2.4 The MVC Pattern — Most Frequent Exam Topic

> [!NOTE]
> **MVC = Model + View + Controller**

- **Model** — Holds the data and business rules. Knows *nothing* about the UI.
- **View** — Renders the Model into a format the user can see (HTML, JSON, PDF).
- **Controller** — Receives user input, updates the Model, and selects a View.

$$
\text{Request} \rightarrow \text{Controller} \rightarrow \text{Model (data + logic)} \rightarrow \text{Controller} \rightarrow \text{View} \rightarrow \text{Response}
$$

### 2.4.1 MVC vs MVP vs MVVM — Know the Difference

| Aspect | MVC | MVP | MVVM |
|---|---|---|---|
| **View knows about** | Model (passive) | Presenter only | ViewModel via bindings |
| **Testability** | Medium | High (Presenter is a pure class) | Very High |
| **Coupling** | Medium | Loose | Very loose |
| **Used in** | Spring, Django, Rails | ASP.NET, GWT | Angular, Vue, WPF |
| **Data flow** | Bidirectional via Controller | One-way View ↔ Presenter | Two-way binding |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU exams, a common trap: *"In MVC, can the View directly update the Model?"* — Answer: **No** (in strict MVC). The View is *passive*; only the Controller can mutate the Model.

---

## 2.5 Testing — Deep Theoretical Framework

### 2.5.1 The V-Model of Testing

The **V-Model** maps every development phase to a corresponding test phase. This is mandatory KTU knowledge.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Requirements} \quad &\longleftrightarrow \quad \text{User Acceptance Testing (UAT)} \\
\text{System Design} \quad &\longleftrightarrow \quad \text{System Testing} \\
\text{Architecture Design} \quad &\longleftrightarrow \quad \text{Integration Testing} \\
\text{Module Design} \quad &\longleftrightarrow \quad \text{Unit Testing} \\
\text{Coding} \quad &\longleftrightarrow \quad \text{Component Testing}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The left arm of the V is *development*; the right arm is *test*. They go in parallel.

### 2.5.2 Levels of Testing

| Level | Scope | Who Does It | When | Tool Examples |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Unit Testing** | Single function / class | Developer | During coding | JUnit (Java), pytest (Python), Jest (JS), Mocha |
| **Integration Testing** | Interaction between modules | Developer / QA | After unit tests | Postman, SoapUI, REST-assured |
| **System Testing** | The whole application | QA team | Pre-release | Selenium, Cypress, JMeter (perf) |
| **Acceptance Testing (UAT)** | Business requirements | End-user / client | Before go-live | Manual + automated regression suite |

### 2.5.3 Types of Testing

| Type | What It Checks |
|---|---|
| **Functional** | Does each feature work as per the spec? |
| **Non-Functional** | Performance, security, usability, accessibility |
| **Performance Testing** | Load, stress, spike, soak, scalability |
| **Security Testing** | SQL injection, XSS, CSRF, broken auth, OWASP Top 10 |
| **Usability Testing** | Can real users achieve their goals? |
| **Compatibility Testing** | Cross-browser, cross-device, cross-OS |
| **Regression Testing** | Does new code break old features? |
| **Smoke Testing** | Build verification — does it even start? |
| **Sanity Testing** | Narrow, deep test of one specific area |
| **A/B Testing** | Two versions compared for user preference |
| **Exploratory Testing** | Unscripted, learning-based testing |

### 2.5.4 Test-Driven Development (TDD) — Red, Green, Refactor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TDD mantra:** *Write a failing test → make it pass → refactor.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1 (RED)} &: \text{ Write a test for functionality that does not exist. Run it. It fails.} \\
\text{Step 2 (GREEN)} &: \text{ Write the minimum code to make the test pass.} \\
\text{Step 3 (REFACTOR)} &: \text{ Clean up the code while keeping tests green.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.5.5 The Testing Pyramid

The **Testing Pyramid** (Mike Cohn) is the industry standard for test distribution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Top (few, slow, expensive)} &: \text{ E2E / UI Tests} \\
\text{Middle (some)} &: \text{ Integration / API Tests} \\
\text{Bottom (many, fast, cheap)} &: \text{ Unit Tests}
\end{aligned}
$$

A healthy project has roughly **70% unit, 20% integration, 10% E2E**.

### 2.5.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Testing Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Code Coverage** | $\text{Coverage} = \dfrac{\text{Lines Executed}}{\text{Total Lines}} \times 100$ | Percentage of code touched by tests |
| **Defect Density** | $\text{DD} = \dfrac{\text{Defects Found}}{\text{Size (KLOC or FP)}}$ | Defects per thousand lines / function point |
| **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | Average uptime between crashes |
| **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Repairs}}$ | Average time to fix a defect |
| **Availability** | $\text{A} = \dfrac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100$ | Uptime percentage (e.g., 99.9% = "three nines") |
| **Test Effectiveness** | $\text{TE} = \dfrac{\text{Defects Found in Test}}{\text{Defects Found in Test + Defects in Production}}$ | Quality of the test suite |
| **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** | $\text{DRE} = \dfrac{\text{Defects Removed Before Release}}{\text{Defects Removed Before Release + Defects After Release}} \times 100$ | Process effectiveness |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary condition to remember:** Availability of **99.999% ("five nines")** allows only **~5.26 minutes** of downtime per year. This is the gold standard for telecom-grade systems.

---

| Architecture | Best For | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Monolith** | Small team, MVP, < 50k users | Early-stage startups |
| **Layered (3-Tier)** | Traditional web apps | Banking portals, ERPs |
| **MVC / MVP / MVVM** | Frameworks, SPAs | Django, Angular |
| **Microservices** | Large teams, Netflix-scale apps | Amazon, Netflix, Uber |
| **Serverless (FaaS)** | Sporadic, event-driven workloads | AWS Lambda, Vercel Functions |
| **Event-Driven (Pub/Sub)** | Real-time feeds, IoT | Kafka, RabbitMQ |
| **JAMstack** | Static-first sites with APIs | Next.js + Vercel + Headless CMS |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section walks through *worked derivations* and *analytical problem-solving* aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme. Every step is shown in full.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Code Coverage Calculation

> **Problem (KTU-Style):**
> A JavaScript module has **400 lines of executable code**. A test suite runs and executes **320 lines**. Calculate the *code coverage*. Is it acceptable per industry standard?

### Step-by-Step Derivation

We use the formula:

$$
\text{Coverage} = \frac{\text{Lines Executed}}{\text{Total Lines}} \times 100
$$

**Step 1:** Identify the values.
- Lines Executed = 320
- Total Lines = 400

**Step 2:** Substitute.

$$
\text{Coverage} = \frac{320}{400} \times 100
$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the fraction.

$$
\frac{320}{400} = \frac{32}{40} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8
$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by 100.

$$
\text{Coverage} = 0.8 \times 100 = 80\%
$$

**Step 5:** Industry interpretation.
- Industry standard (Google, Microsoft, Amazon): **≥ 80%** for new code.
- Critical systems (medical, aviation): **≥ 95%**.
- Hence, **80% is acceptable for a normal web app but insufficient for a safety-critical system**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Defect Density

> **Problem:** A web project of **15,000 lines** of code (15 KLOC) is released. In the first month, **45 defects** are reported by users. Calculate the **defect density**.

### Derivation

$$
\text{DD} = \frac{\text{Defects Found}}{\text{Size (KLOC)}}
$$

**Step 1:** Substitute.

$$
\text{DD} = \frac{45}{15}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute.

$$
\text{DD} = 3 \text{ defects per KLOC}
$$

**Step 3:** Industry benchmark interpretation.
- **< 1 defect/KLOC** → Excellent (world-class).
- **1–5 defects/KLOC** → Typical for medium-quality web apps.
- **> 10 defects/KLOC** → Poor quality; release should be blocked.

Hence **3 defects/KLOC is acceptable** but improvement is needed.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Availability Calculation

> **Problem:** A web server has MTTF = **900 hours** and MTTR = **1 hour**. Calculate the annual availability. How many minutes of downtime per year does this allow?

### Derivation

**Step 1:** Apply the formula.

$$
A = \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute.

$$
A = \frac{900}{900 + 1} \times 100 = \frac{900}{901} \times 100
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the ratio.

$$
\frac{900}{901} \approx 0.998890...
$$

**Step 4:** Convert to percentage.

$$
A \approx 99.8890\%
$$

**Step 5:** Round to "nines" notation.

$$
A \approx 99.89\% \approx \text{"three nines"}.
$$

**Step 6:** Calculate annual downtime.

$$
\text{Total Year (hours)} = 365 \times 24 = 8760 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
\text{Downtime (hours)} = 8760 \times (1 - 0.99889) = 8760 \times 0.00111
$$

$$
\text{Downtime} \approx 9.72 \text{ hours/year}
$$

**Step 7:** Convert to minutes.

$$
9.72 \times 60 \approx 583 \text{ minutes/year} \approx 9.7 \text{ hours/year}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Quick mental math:** Each "nine" multiplies the downtime precision by 10.
> - "Two nines" (99%) → 3.65 days/year.
> - "Three nines" (99.9%) → 8.77 hours/year.
> - "Four nines" (99.99%) → 52.6 minutes/year.
> - "Five nines" (99.999%) → 5.26 minutes/year.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Test Effectiveness

> **Problem:** Before release, QA found **90 defects**. After release, users found **10 defects** within a month. Calculate the **Test Effectiveness (TE)**.

### Derivation

$$
\text{TE} = \frac{\text{Defects Found in Test}}{\text{Defects Found in Test + Defects in Production}}
$$

**Step 1:** Substitute.

$$
\text{TE} = \frac{90}{90 + 10} = \frac{90}{100}
$$

**Step 2:** Convert to percentage.

$$
\text{TE} = 90\%
$$

**Step 3:** Interpretation.
- A TE of **≥ 95%** is excellent.
- **90% is good** but the team should investigate why 10% slipped through.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Pattern

> **Problem-Style Question (KTU):**
> *"Match the following web scenarios with the most suitable design pattern."*

| # | Web Scenario | Best Pattern | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Only one DB connection pool must exist globally | **Singleton** | Prevents multiple connection pools from exhausting resources |
| 2 | Express middleware that adds CORS headers to every request | **Decorator** | Wraps the request/response with additional behavior |
| 3 | A button click must update multiple UI panels in real time | **Observer** | One event triggers many subscribers |
| 4 | Switch between Stripe, PayPal, and Razorpay at runtime | **Strategy** | Family of interchangeable algorithms |
| 5 | Hide the complexity of multiple API calls behind one simple method | **Facade** | Simplified interface over a complex subsystem |
| 6 | Lazy-load an expensive chart library only when the chart view is rendered | **Proxy** (Virtual Proxy) | Defers creation until needed |
| 7 | Build an SQL query piece by piece with optional WHERE clauses | **Builder** | Step-by-step construction of a complex object |
| 8 | Undo/Redo functionality in a text editor | **Command** | Each action is an encapsulated object with `execute`/`undo` |
| 9 | Single entry point (`index.php`) that routes all HTTP requests | **Front Controller** | Centralized request handling |
| 10 | Abstract DB access so business code doesn't know it's MySQL | **DAO / Repository** | Decouples persistence from business logic |

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Testing Level

| # | Web Scenario | Right Testing Level | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Verify a `calculateTax()` function returns the correct GST amount | **Unit Test** | Single function, no I/O |
| 2 | Ensure the Order Service calls the Payment Service correctly | **Integration Test** | Cross-module contract |
| 3 | Confirm the entire checkout flow from cart to invoice | **System / E2E Test** | Full workflow |
| 4 | Validate that 10,000 concurrent users can place orders | **Performance / Load Test** | Non-functional, scale |
| 5 | Check if a SQL injection vulnerability exists in a search field | **Security Test** | OWASP Top 10 |
| 6 | Verify the app works on Chrome, Firefox, Safari, and Edge | **Compatibility Test** | Cross-browser |
| 7 | After a bug fix, re-test all old features to ensure no breakage | **Regression Test** | Prevent regressions |
| 8 | Quickly check if the latest build is even installable | **Smoke Test** | Build verification |
| 9 | User confirms the app meets the original business needs | **UAT / Acceptance** | Business validation |
| 10 | Compare version A vs version B of a homepage to see which converts better | **A/B Test** | Data-driven UX |

---

## 3.7 Derivation of the Testing Pyramid Ratio

> **Question:** *"Why is the testing pyramid base 70% unit, 20% integration, 10% E2E?"*

### Reasoning

**Step 1 — Speed:**
- Unit tests: **milliseconds** per test.
- Integration tests: **seconds** per test.
- E2E tests: **minutes** per test (browser startup, network).

**Step 2 — Cost of maintenance:**
- Unit tests rarely break when code is refactored (test the *contract*, not the *implementation*).
- E2E tests break on every UI change (CSS tweak, button rename).

**Step 3 — Reliability:**
- Unit tests are deterministic; no network, no race conditions.
- E2E tests are flaky (timeouts, animations, async waits).

**Step 4 — Coverage efficiency:**
- A single integration test covers 3–5 units at once.
- One unit test only covers one unit.
- Hence, fewer integration tests are needed.

**Step 5 — Empirical industry data:**
Google's testing blog and Microsoft's empirical studies show the **70/20/10** ratio yields the **best feedback loop** for the **least cost**.
