# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Principle of Layering in Web Application Design. Mention any two benefits. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Principle of Layering** is the architectural practice of dividing a web application into a stack of **horizontally separated**, **logically distinct** layers — typically *Presentation*, *Application (Business Logic)*, and *Data* — where each layer has a single responsibility and communicates **only** with the layer immediately below it.

**Two benefits (1.5 marks each):**

1. **Separation of Concerns** — Each layer handles one responsibility (e.g., UI rendering, business rules, persistence), making the system easier to understand and modify.
2. **Independent Testability & Deployability** — The business layer can be unit-tested without the UI; the front-end framework can be swapped without touching the back-end, accelerating development cycles.

---

### Question 2: What is the Singleton Design Pattern? Give one web use case. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Singleton Pattern** is a *creational* design pattern that ensures a class has **exactly one instance** and provides a **global point of access** to it. The constructor is made private; a static method returns the single shared instance, typically created lazily and protected against multi-threaded races.

**Web use case (1.5 marks):**
- **Database Connection Pool:** A web server must use a *single* connection pool to avoid exhausting DB resources. Multiple pools would cause memory leaks and connection storms.
- **Logger:** A single shared logger instance writes to one log file from all modules.
- **Configuration Object:** One shared `Config` object holding API keys, DB URLs, and feature flags.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Rule:** Each question provides TWO alternatives. *Answer ANY ONE full question.*

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `CHOICE 1`  `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

> **(a)** Explain the **3-Tier Architecture** of a web application with a neat diagram. Describe the responsibilities of each tier. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare **MVC**, **MVP**, and **MVVM** patterns. State one framework that uses each. **(7 marks)**

#### (a) Model Answer — 3-Tier Architecture (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
The 3-Tier Architecture divides a web application into three logical and often physical layers: **Presentation**, **Application (Business Logic)**, and **Data**. Each tier is independent and communicates only with the layer below it.

**Diagram (2 marks):**

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│   PRESENTATION TIER (Tier 1)        │
│   HTML, CSS, JS, React, Angular     │
│   - Renders UI                      │
│   - Captures user input             │
│   - Client-side validation          │
└─────────────────────────────────────┘
                  │
                  ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│   APPLICATION TIER (Tier 2)         │
│   Java/Spring, Python/Django, Node  │
│   - Business rules                  │
│   - Server-side validation          │
│   - Workflow orchestration          │
│   - Authentication & authorization  │
└─────────────────────────────────────┘
                  │
                  ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│   DATA TIER (Tier 3)                │
│   MySQL, PostgreSQL, MongoDB, Redis │
│   - Persistent storage              │
│   - CRUD operations                 │
│   - Transactions                    │
└─────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Responsibilities (4 marks):**

| Tier | Responsibilities |
|---|---|
| **Presentation** | • Renders the user interface using HTML/CSS/JS.<br>• Captures and validates client input (e.g., email format).<br>• Sends HTTP requests to the Application tier.<br>• Displays responses (HTML pages, JSON for AJAX). |
| **Application** | • Enforces business rules (e.g., *"discount applies only if cart > ₹500"*).<br>• Performs server-side validation and sanitization.<br>• Manages user sessions, authentication, and authorization.<br>• Orchestrates workflows (order placement, payment).<br>• Acts as the *only* gateway to the Data tier. |
| **Data** | • Provides persistent storage of entities.<br>• Handles CRUD via SQL/NoSQL queries.<br>• Ensures ACID transactions and data integrity.<br>• Manages indexing, replication, and backups. |

**Strict Rule (extra point):** The Presentation tier **never** talks to the Data tier directly — all requests go through the Application tier. Violations break the architecture.

#### (b) Model Answer — MVC vs MVP vs MVVM (7 marks)

| Aspect | **MVC** | **MVP** | **MVVM** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Full Form** | Model-View-Controller | Model-View-Presenter | Model-View-ViewModel |
| **Components** | Model, View, Controller | Model, View, Presenter | Model, View, ViewModel |
| **Role of View** | Passive; updates only via Controller | Passive; talks *only* to Presenter | Active; binds to ViewModel |
| **Communication** | Controller mediates Model ↔ View | Presenter mediates everything | Two-way data binding auto-syncs |
| **Testability** | Medium (View is hard to mock) | High (Presenter is a plain class) | Very High (ViewModel is testable) |
| **Coupling** | Medium | Loose | Very loose |
| **Framework Example** | Django, Spring MVC, Ruby on Rails | ASP.NET Web Forms, GWT | Angular, Vue.js, WPF |

**One-line summary (extra point):**
- *MVC* — View is dumb, Controller is the brain.
- *MVP* — Presenter is the brain, View is even dumber.
- *MVVM* — View is smart, ViewModel is the brain, they auto-sync.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `CHOICE 2`  `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

> **(a)** What is **Software Design Pattern**? Explain the **Observer** and **Strategy** patterns with web-context examples. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **V-Model of Testing** with a diagram. List any **four types of testing** with one example each. **(7 marks)**

#### (a) Model Answer — Observer & Strategy Patterns (7 marks)

**Definition of Design Pattern (1 mark):**
A *Software Design Pattern* is a **reusable, proven, well-documented solution** to a recurring problem in software design. Patterns are *templates* (not finished code) that capture the essence of a working solution and can be applied across many contexts.

**Observer Pattern (3 marks):**
- **Intent:** Define a **one-to-many** dependency so that when one object (the *Subject*) changes state, all its dependents (*Observers*) are notified automatically.
- **Participants:** Subject, ConcreteSubject, Observer, ConcreteObserver.
- **Web Example:** In a *chat application*, when a new message is posted (Subject), the UI message panel, the unread-badge counter, the push-notification service, and the analytics logger (Observers) all get notified and updated in real time.
- **Implementation hint (Node.js):**

```javascript
const EventEmitter = require('events');
const chat = new EventEmitter();
chat.on('newMessage', msg => console.log('UI updated:', msg));
chat.on('newMessage', msg => console.log('Badge incremented'));
chat.emit('newMessage', 'Hello KTU!');
```

**Strategy Pattern (3 marks):**
- **Intent:** Define a *family of algorithms*, encapsulate each one, and make them **interchangeable at runtime** without altering the client.
- **Participants:** Strategy interface, ConcreteStrategyA/B/C, Context.
- **Web Example:** An e-commerce site supports **Stripe**, **PayPal**, and **Razorpay**. The `PaymentContext` accepts a `PaymentStrategy` interface; the user picks one at checkout. Switching gateways = swapping a strategy object, *no change* to the order code.
- **Why use it:** Avoids massive `if-else`/`switch` blocks; aligns with the *Open/Closed Principle* (open for extension, closed for modification).

#### (b) Model Answer — V-Model & Testing Types (7 marks)

**V-Model Definition (1 mark):**
The **V-Model** (Verification & Validation Model) is a software development lifecycle where **every development phase has a corresponding testing phase**. The left arm represents *development/decomposition*; the right arm represents *testing/recursion*.

**V-Model Diagram (2 marks):**

```
Requirements ──────────────────────── UAT
   │                                    ▲
System Design ──────────────────── System Test
   │                                    ▲
Architecture Design ──────────── Integration Test
   │                                    ▲
Module Design ────────────────── Unit Test
   │                                    ▲
Coding ─────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Explanation (2 marks):**
- *Requirements ↔ UAT:* Validates business needs.
- *System Design ↔ System Test:* Validates the whole app.
- *Architecture Design ↔ Integration Test:* Validates inter-module contracts.
- *Module Design ↔ Unit Test:* Validates individual functions/classes.
- Tests are planned **in parallel** with development, not after.

**Four Types of Testing (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

| # | Type | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Unit Testing** | Testing a `calculateTotal()` function returns the correct sum for a given cart. |
| 2 | **Integration Testing** | Verifying that the *Order Service* correctly calls the *Payment Service* and persists the result. |
| 3 | **Performance (Load) Testing** | Simulating **10,000 concurrent users** to check if response time stays < 2 seconds. |
| 4 | **Security Testing** | Attempting **SQL injection** (`' OR 1=1 --`) in a login form to bypass authentication. |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Traps**
> 1. **Confusing "tier" and "layer":** *Tier* = physical separation (different machine/process). *Layer* = logical separation (same machine, different module). Many students use them interchangeably — *don't*.
> 2. **Listing patterns without web context:** Naming Singleton/Observer/Strategy without a *web example* loses 50% marks. Always pair the pattern with a **web scenario**.
> 3. **Forgetting the *passive* nature of View in MVC:** Writing *"View updates Model directly"* in MVC gets **zero** marks.
> 4. **V-Model diagram reversed:** Drawing the V upside-down or putting test phases on the *left* arm costs full marks on the diagram.
> 5. **No units in metrics:** Writing *"coverage = 80"* without the **%** sign is considered incomplete. Always write units (%, defects/KLOC, hours, minutes).
> 6. **Mixing TDD and Waterfall:** TDD is *test-first*; Waterfall is *test-last*. Don't claim TDD is part of Waterfall.
> 7. **Singleton is *not* a global variable:** A Singleton enforces *one* instance via a *private constructor*. A global variable has no such guarantee.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print or pin this section before every KTU exam.**

### ✅ Real World Web Software Design
- [ ] Web design is a *bridge* between analysis and implementation.
- [ ] Deliverables: **SRS → HLD → LLD → Code → Test Report**.
- [ ] Quality attributes: scalability, maintainability, reusability, testability, security, performance, usability, portability.
- [ ] Feasibility = Technical + Economic + Operational + Schedule + Legal.
- [ ] Web design is *phased*, not ad-hoc.

### ✅ Principle of Layering
- [ ] 3-Tier = Presentation + Application + Data.
- [ ] N-Tier extends with API Gateway, Cache, Message Queue.
- [ ] **Rule:** Layer talks *only* to the layer below.
- [ ] Tier = *physical*; Layer = *logical*.
- [ ] Benefits: separation of concerns, parallel dev, testability, security.
- [ ] Drawbacks: latency, complexity, over-engineering risk.

### ✅ Design Patterns
- [ ] **Creational** — Singleton, Factory, Builder, Prototype.
- [ ] **Structural** — Adapter, Decorator, Facade, Proxy.
- [ ] **Behavioral** — Observer, Strategy, Command, Iterator.
- [ ] **Architectural** — MVC, MVP, MVVM, Front Controller, DAO/Repository, Layered.
- [ ] **MVC** is the most-asked pattern. View is *passive*; Controller is *active*.
- [ ] **Singleton** = one global instance (e.g., DB pool, Logger).
- [ ] **Observer** = one-to-many notification (e.g., WebSocket chat, DOM events).
- [ ] **Strategy** = swappable algorithm (e.g., payment gateways).
- [ ] **Decorator** = add behavior dynamically (e.g., Express middleware).
- [ ] **Facade** = simplified interface over complex subsystem (e.g., jQuery).
- [ ] **Front Controller** = single entry point (e.g., `index.php`, Express `app.use`).

### ✅ Testing
- [ ] Testing = *finding defects* (Glenford Myers).
- [ ] **V-Model:** Each dev phase ↔ a corresponding test phase.
- [ ] **Testing Pyramid:** 70% Unit, 20% Integration, 10% E2E.
- [ ] **TDD Cycle:** RED → GREEN → REFACTOR.
- [ ] Testing types: Unit, Integration, System, UAT, Performance, Security, Compatibility, Regression, Smoke, Sanity, A/B, Exploratory.
- [ ] **Code Coverage** = (Executed Lines / Total Lines) × 100.
- [ ] **Defect Density** = Defects / KLOC. Industry good: < 1–5.
- [ ] **Availability** = MTTF / (MTTF + MTTR) × 100.
- [ ] **Five nines (99.999%)** = ~5.26 minutes downtime/year.
- [ ] **DRE** (Defect Removal Efficiency) measures pre-release process quality.

### ✅ Exam-Specific Traps to Avoid
- [ ] Don't confuse *tier* with *layer*.
- [ ] Always pair a pattern with a **web example**.
- [ ] Always include **units** in metric answers (%, hours, defects/KLOC).
- [ ] Never draw the **V-Model upside down**.
- [ ] Never call *Singleton* a "global variable".

---

> **End of Module 4 — Web Application Design Notes**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme | OECST832 — Web Programming*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Web Services: Overview of Web Services, SOAP Services, REST Services, An Example Web Service, Web Server, Hosting Options
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECST832 - WEB PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Web Services: Overview of Web Services, SOAP Services, REST Services, An Example Web Service, Web Server, Hosting Options # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Overview of Web Services A **Web Service** is a standardized, interoperable, language-independent software system designed to support machine-to-machine interaction over a network. According to the W3C (World Wide Web Consortium) definition, a web service is "a software system designed to support interoperable machine-to-machine interaction over a network." > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A web service is a software application component that communicates over open, standard protocols like HTTP, SOAP, XML, JSON, and WSDL to enable interoperability between heterogeneous systems and platforms. ### Key Characteristics of Web Services - **Platform & Language Independent:** Built on open standards (XML, JSON, HTTP). - **Loosely Coupled:** Consumer and provider have minimal knowledge of each other. - **Self-Describing:** Uses WSDL (Web Services Description Language) or OpenAPI. - **Discoverable:** Can be located via UDDI (Universal Description, Discovery, and Integration) registries. - **Stateless:** Each request is independent and self-contained. - **Composable:** Multiple services can be orchestrated into composite applications. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Restaurant Waiter > [!NOTE] > **Think of a Web Service as a Waiter in a Restaurant 🍽️** > > - **You (the Client)** sit at the table and want food (data/functionality). > - **The Kitchen (the Server/Provider)** prepares the food but never talks to you directly. > - **The Waiter (the Web Service)** takes your order in a standard format (menu), goes to the kitchen, returns with the dish, and serves it. > > The waiter uses a **standard protocol** (taking orders in English, regardless of your native language), makes the kitchen **discoverable** (you don't need to know the chef's name), and returns the response in a **structured format** (the plated dish). In technical terms: | Restaurant Element | Web Service Equivalent | |---|---| | Customer | Client Application | | Waiter | Web Service / API | | Order (in English) | XML / JSON Request | | Kitchen | Server / Database | | Food Delivered | XML / JSON Response | | Menu | WSDL / OpenAPI Document | --- ## 1.3 Types of Web Services There are two dominant web service paradigms covered in the KTU 2024 syllabus: ### A. SOAP (Simple Object Access Protocol) A **protocol-based**, heavyweight, XML-driven web service that uses WSDL for service description and typically operates over HTTP, SMTP, or TCP. ### B. REST (Representational State Transfer) An **architectural style**, lightweight, resource-oriented web service that leverages standard HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) and is most commonly formatted using JSON. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** SOAP is a *protocol*; REST is an *architectural style*. This distinction is a frequently asked 3-mark question in KTU board exams. --- ## 1.4 SOAP Services — Definition **SOAP (Simple Object Access Protocol)** is a strictly defined XML-based messaging protocol specification for exchanging structured information in the implementation of web services. It relies on: - **XML** for message format - **WSDL** for service description - **UDDI** for service discovery - **HTTP/SMTP** for transport --- ## 1.5 REST Services — Definition **REST (Representational State Transfer)** is an architectural style introduced by **Roy Fielding** in his 2000 doctoral dissertation. It treats every piece of data on the server as a *resource* identified by a URL, and operations are performed using standard HTTP verbs. > [!NOTE] > **Core Insight:** REST is not a protocol; it is a *set of architectural constraints* (statelessness, uniform interface, cacheability, layered system, code-on-demand) that, when applied, produce a web service. --- ## 1.6 An Example Web Service A classic pedagogical example used in KTU textbooks is the **Temperature Conversion Web Service** or the **Currency Converter Web Service**. ### Example: Temperature Conversion Service - **Endpoint URL:** `https://api.example.com/convert` - **Resource:** Temperature data - **Operation:** Convert Celsius to Fahrenheit - **Mathematical Relation:** $$ F = \left(C \times \frac{9}{5}\right) + 32 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this example matters for KTU:** It demonstrates a stateless mathematical operation exposed over HTTP — the exact kind of "small, useful web service" asked in Part A viva questions. --- ## 1.7 Web Server — Definition A **Web Server** is a software (or the hardware hosting it) that processes incoming HTTP/HTTPS requests from clients (browsers, mobile apps, other services) and returns the appropriate response. Popular web servers include: - **Apache HTTP Server** (open-source, by Apache Foundation) - **NGINX** (high-performance, by F5 Networks) - **Microsoft IIS** (Internet Information Services) - **LiteSpeed** (high-performance drop-in for Apache) - **Tomcat** (Java Servlet container by Apache) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Note:** A web server handles **static content** (HTML, CSS, images) and forwards dynamic requests to application servers (e.g., Node.js, Tomcat, WildFly). --- ## 1.8 Hosting Options Web applications and services can be deployed across various hosting options: | Hosting Type | Description | Example Providers | |---|---|---| | **Shared Hosting** | Multiple sites on one server | Bluehost, HostGator | | **VPS (Virtual Private Server)** | Virtualized server partition | DigitalOcean, Linode | | **Dedicated Server** | Entire physical server for one client | Rackspace, OVH | | **Cloud Hosting (PaaS)** | Platform-managed scalable environment | AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Azure App Service, Google App Engine | | **IaaS (Cloud VMs)** | Rent raw VMs in the cloud | AWS EC2, Azure VM, GCP Compute Engine | | **Serverless** | Run code in response to events (no server management) | AWS Lambda, Azure Functions, Google Cloud Functions | | **Containerized** | Run inside Docker/Kubernetes clusters | Docker Hub, Amazon EKS, GKE | | **On-Premise** | Locally hosted infrastructure | Corporate data centers | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 SOAP Architecture — The Operational Stack SOAP-based web services are built on a layered architecture. Let us break down each layer: ### Layer 1: Service Transport Layer - SOAP messages can be transported over **HTTP, HTTPS, SMTP, TCP, or JMS**. - HTTP is the most common transport. ### Layer 2: XML Messaging Layer - Defines the **SOAP envelope**: a complete SOAP message is wrapped in an XML envelope. - Mandatory structure: `` → `
` (optional) + `` (mandatory). ### Layer 3: Service Description Layer (WSDL) - **WSDL (Web Services Description Language)** is an XML document that describes: - What the service does - The operations it offers - The input/output parameters - The protocol/endpoint - A WSDL file has two parts: **abstract definitions** (portType, message, types) and **concrete definitions** (binding, service, port). ### Layer 4: Service Discovery Layer (UDDI) - **UDDI (Universal Description, Discovery, and Integration)** is a directory for publishing and discovering web services. - In modern practice, UDDI has been largely replaced by **API Gateways** and **Service Registries** (e.g., Consul, Eureka). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Mnemonic — "S-T-X-D": SOAP Transport, XML messaging, Description (WSDL), Discovery (UDDI).** --- ## 2.2 SOAP Message Anatomy A SOAP message is a well-formed XML document with the following mandatory structure: ```xml 100 ``` ### Why is the Envelope Mandatory? - The `` is the **root element** — SOAP processors use the namespace to identify a SOAP message. - Without the envelope, the message is not a valid SOAP message, even if it is well-formed XML. --- ## 2.3 REST Architecture — The Operational Constraints REST is governed by **6 architectural constraints** introduced by Fielding: | # | Constraint | Meaning | KTU Significance | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Client-Server** | Separation of concerns between UI and data storage | Enables independent evolution | | 2 | **Stateless** | Each request contains all info; server stores no client state | Simplifies scalability | | 3 | **Cacheable** | Responses must define themselves as cacheable or not | Improves performance | | 4 | **Uniform Interface** | Standardized resource identification (URI) and methods (HTTP verbs) | Decouples client and service | | 5 | **Layered System** | Client cannot tell if connected to end server or intermediary | Supports load balancers, proxies | | 6 | **Code-on-Demand (Optional)** | Server can send executable code (e.g., JavaScript) | Rarely used; most APIs skip this | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Question Pattern:** "List and explain the constraints of RESTful architecture." (5-mark question, Module 4.) --- ## 2.4 REST Verbs and Idempotency | HTTP Method | CRUD Operation | Idempotent? | Safe? | |---|---|---|---| | `GET` | Read | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | | `POST` | Create | ❌ No | ❌ No | | `PUT` | Update/Replace | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | | `PATCH` | Partial Update | ❌ No | ❌ No | | `DELETE` | Delete | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | > [!NOTE] > **Definitions:** > - **Idempotent:** Calling the operation N times produces the same result as calling it once. > - **Safe:** Calling the operation does not modify server state (side-effect-free). --- ## 2.5 SOAP vs REST — KTU Comparison Cheat Sheet | Feature | SOAP | REST | |---|---|---| | **Type** | Protocol | Architectural Style | | **Data Format** | XML only | JSON, XML, YAML, plain text | | **Transport** | HTTP, SMTP, TCP, JMS | HTTP/HTTPS only | | **Contract** | WSDL (mandatory) | OpenAPI / Swagger (optional) | | **Statefulness** | Can be stateful | Stateless | | **Security** | WS-Security, SSL, SAML | HTTPS, OAuth2, JWT | | **Performance** | Slower (heavy XML parsing) | Faster (lightweight JSON) | | **Bandwidth** | High (verbose XML) | Low (compact JSON) | | **Error Handling** | Built-in `` element | HTTP status codes (404, 500) | | **Tooling** | Heavy (e.g., JAX-WS, CXF) | Light (e.g., Express.js, Flask) | | **Use Case** | Banking, Enterprise, SOAP-based legacy systems | Public APIs, Mobile, IoT, Microservices | | **Discovery** | UDDI | API Gateway / Service Registry | > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often answer "REST is a protocol" — this is **wrong** and costs marks. REST is an *architectural style*; SOAP is a *protocol*. --- ## 2.6 Web Server Functions — Engineering Utility A web server performs these engineering functions: 1. **HTTP Request Handling:** Parses incoming HTTP requests (method, URI, headers, body). 2. **Static Content Serving:** Delivers HTML, CSS, JS, images directly from disk. 3. **Dynamic Content Forwarding:** Uses CGI, FastCGI, or reverse proxies to delegate dynamic requests to app servers. 4. **SSL/TLS Termination:** Handles HTTPS encryption/decryption. 5. **Load Balancing:** Distributes requests across multiple backend servers (NGINX, HAProxy). 6. **Compression:** Gzip/Brotli compression to reduce bandwidth. 7. **Caching:** Stores frequent responses to reduce load. 8. **Logging & Monitoring:** Records access logs, error logs, and metrics. ### Real-World Utility - **E-commerce sites** use NGINX to serve millions of static product images. - **Streaming platforms (Netflix, YouTube)** use NGINX to handle massive concurrent connections. - **Banking APIs** use Apache + WSO2 to provide SOAP-based, WS-Secured transactions. --- ## 2.7 Hosting Decision Matrix — KTU Quick Reference | Factor | Shared | VPS | Dedicated | Cloud (PaaS) | Serverless | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Cost** | Low | Medium | High | Pay-per-use | Pay-per-execution | | **Control** | Low | High | Full | Medium | None | | **Scalability** | None | Manual | Manual | Auto | Auto (infinite) | | **Maintenance** | Provider | You | You | Provider | Provider | | **Best For** | Small sites | Mid-size apps | High-traffic legacy | Modern web apps | Event-driven tasks | | **KTU Example** | Student blog | College site | Bank core | Heroku app | AWS Lambda trigger | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Viva Tip:** Be ready to explain when to choose **serverless** (spiky, unpredictable workloads) vs **PaaS** (steady, predictable traffic) vs **IaaS** (custom OS, special compliance). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: The Temperature Conversion Web Service (Mathematical Foundation) The Temperature Conversion Web Service is a frequently used illustrative example. Let us derive the underlying equation formally. ### Given - $C$ = Temperature in Celsius (input) - $F$ = Temperature in Fahrenheit (output) - The physical relationship between the two scales ### Step 1: Identify the Fixed Points - At the **freezing point of water** at standard atmospheric pressure ($1\,\text{atm}$): - $C = 0\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ corresponds to $F = 32\,^{\circ}\text{F}$ - At the **boiling point of water** at standard atmospheric pressure ($1\,\text{atm}$): - $C = 100\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ corresponds to $F = 212\,^{\circ}\text{F}$ ### Step 2: Establish Linearity Temperature scales are linear. Thus, $F$ is a linear function of $C$: $$ F = m \cdot C + b $$ where $m$ is the slope and $b$ is the y-intercept. ### Step 3: Calculate the Slope $m$ $$ m = \frac{F_2 - F_1}{C_2 - C_1} = \frac{212 - 32}{100 - 0} = \frac{180}{100} = \frac{9}{5} $$ ### Step 4: Calculate the Intercept $b$ Substituting point $(0, 32)$: $$ 32 = m \cdot 0 + b \implies b = 32 $$ ### Step 5: Combine to Form the Final Equation $$ F = \frac{9}{5} C + 32 $$ > This is the equation implemented inside the Temperature Conversion Web Service. The function is **pure** (no side effects), **idempotent**, and **stateless** — making it an ideal candidate for a REST endpoint. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example: REST API Request/Response Cycle ### Scenario A client sends a request to convert $C = 100$ to Fahrenheit via a REST API. ### Step 1: Client Constructs the Request ```http GET /api/convert?from=C&to=F&value=100 HTTP/1.1 Host: api.tempconv.example.com Accept: application/json ``` ### Step 2: Server Receives and Routes The web server (e.g., NGINX) forwards the request to the application logic, which: 1. Parses query parameters: `from=C`, `to=F`, `value=100` 2. Applies the conversion formula. ### Step 3: Apply the Formula $$ F = \frac{9}{5} \cdot 100 + 32 $$ $$ F = 180 + 32 = 212\,^{\circ}\text{F} $$ ### Step 4: Server Returns the JSON Response ```http HTTP/1.1 200 OK Content-Type: application/json { "input": { "value": 100, "unit": "C" }, "output": { "value": 212, "unit": "F" }, "formula": "F = (9/5) * C + 32" } ``` ### Step 5: Client Renders / Uses the Result The client displays: *"100°C = 212°F"*. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: SOAP Request-Response Cycle For a SOAP-based equivalent of the same service: ### Step 1: Client Sends a SOAP Request ```xml POST /TemperatureService HTTP/1.1 Host: www.example.org Content-Type: text/xml; charset="utf-8" Content-Length: 350 SOAPAction: "http://example.org/CelsiusToFahrenheit" 100 ``` ### Step 2: Server Applies Same Conversion Logic Same formula: $$ F = \frac{9}{5} C + 32 = 212 $$ ### Step 3: Server Returns a SOAP Response ```xml HTTP/1.1 200 OK Content-Type: text/xml; charset="utf-8" 212 ``` ### Step 4: Analytical Comparison | Aspect | SOAP (this example) | REST (this example) | |---|---|---| | Bytes Transmitted (approx.) | $\approx 480$ bytes | $\approx 250$ bytes | | Human-Readable | Verbose XML | Compact JSON | | Parsing Overhead | High (XML parser) | Low (JSON parser) | | Service Description | WSDL required | OpenAPI optional | --- ## 3.4 Worked Example: SOAP Fault Handling If the client passes a non-numeric value (e.g., `"abc"`), the server must return a structured fault. ### Fault Response ```xml HTTP/1.1 500 Internal Server Error Content-Type: text/xml; charset="utf-8" soap:Client Invalid input: not a number INVALID_INPUT Expected a numeric value for Celsius. ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** SOAP provides built-in error semantics via the `` element. REST instead uses **HTTP status codes** (400 Bad Request, 422 Unprocessable Entity) and may include an error body in JSON. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Web Server Performance Metrics When selecting a web server, these engineering metrics matter: | Metric | Definition | Typical Value (Modern Server) | |---|---|---| | **Throughput** | Requests handled per second | $10{,}000$–$100{,}000$ req/s | | **Latency** | Time to first byte | $1$–$10\,\text{ms}$ (p99) | | **Concurrent Connections** | Simultaneous open TCP connections | $1{,}000$–$1{,}000{,}000$ | | **Memory per Connection** | RAM consumed per idle connection | $\approx 2\text{–}10\,\text{KB}$ | | **Static File Throughput** | MB/s for HTML/CSS/JS | $500$–$5{,}000\,\text{MB/s}$ | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Real-World Note:** NGINX uses an **event-driven, asynchronous** architecture, while Apache traditionally uses a **process-per-connection** model. This is why NGINX typically handles $10\times$ more concurrent connections at the same memory footprint. --- ## 3.6 Worked Example: Hosting Cost Calculation (Serverless vs VPS) ### Scenario A college project website receives **100,000 requests/month**, each running for **200 ms**, with **128 MB memory**. ### Serverless Cost (AWS Lambda model) - Free tier: 1,000,000 requests/month + 400,000 GB-seconds. - Compute: $(100{,}000 \times 0.2\,\text{s} \times 0.128\,\text{GB}) = 2{,}560\,\text{GB-seconds}$ - Both metrics stay under the free tier ⇒ **Total: $\approx \$0$/month**. ### VPS Cost - Cheapest DigitalOcean droplet: **\$4/month** (always running). - Total: **\$48/year**. ### Trade-off - Serverless = **\$0** but with cold-start latency ($100$–$500\,\text{ms}$ on first invocation). - VPS = **\$48/year** with consistent low latency but 24/7 resource usage. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Verdict:** Choose serverless for spiky/unpredictable traffic; choose VPS for steady traffic requiring low, consistent latency. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Web Service Architecture — High-Level Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB clientA[CLIENT A
Web Browser] clientB[CLIENT B
Mobile App] clientC[CLIENT C
Other Service] net[NETWORK LAYER
Internet / Intranet] wsServer[WEB SERVER
NGINX / Apache
Routes Requests] apiGw[API GATEWAY
Auth Throttling Logging] soapEng[SOAP ENGINE
JAX-WS Apache CXF] restEng[REST ENGINE
Express Spring Boot] db[(DATABASE
MySQL PostgreSQL)] cache[(CACHE
Redis Memcached)] clientA --> net clientB --> net clientC --> net net --> wsServer wsServer --> apiGw apiGw --> soapEng apiGw --> restEng soapEng --> db restEng --> db restEng --> cache soapEng --> cache ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to read this diagram:** Clients send HTTP requests → Web Server routes them → API Gateway handles cross-cutting concerns (auth, throttling) → The appropriate engine (SOAP or REST) processes the request → Data is fetched from the database (with optional caching for speed). --- ## 4.2 SOAP Communication Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant C as Client participant S as SOAP Server participant W as WSDL Document participant D as Database C->>W: 1. Fetch WSDL to discover service W-->>C: 2. Returns WSDL XML describing operations C->>S: 3. Sends SOAP Request (XML Envelope over HTTP POST) S->>S: 4. Parses Envelope Header and Body S->>D: 5. Executes business logic / query D-->>S: 6. Returns data S->>S: 7. Wraps result in SOAP Response Envelope S-->>C: 8. Returns SOAP Response or Fault ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Step — WSDL First:** In SOAP, the client **first downloads the WSDL** to know what operations exist and how to call them. REST has no mandatory equivalent; clients simply know the API contract from documentation. --- ## 4.3 REST API Communication Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant C as Client participant S as REST Server participant DB as Database participant Cache as Cache C->>S: 1. GET /api/users/42 HTTP/1.1 S->>S: 2. Extract resource ID from URI S->>Cache: 3. Check if user 42 is cached alt Cache Hit Cache-->>S: 4a. Return cached user data else Cache Miss S->>DB: 4b. Query SELECT FROM users WHERE id=42 DB-->>S: 5. Return user data S->>Cache: 6. Store user 42 in cache end S->>S: 7. Serialize data as JSON S-->>C: 8. HTTP 200 OK with JSON body ``` > [!NOTE] > **REST Key Insight:** The server is **stateless** — it doesn't remember this client between requests. The cache is a *server-side optimization* and is not part of the REST contract. --- ## 4.4 Web Server Request Processing Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR req[Incoming HTTP Request] --> parse[Phase 1 Parse Request Line
Method URI HTTP Version] parse --> headers[Phase 2 Read Headers
Host Cookie Content-Type] headers --> auth[Phase 3 Authentication
Basic OAuth JWT] auth --> routing[Phase 4 Routing
Static File vs App Server] routing --> decision{Static Content?} decision -- Yes --> static[Serve from Disk] decision -- No --> dynamic[Forward to App Server
Node Tomcat Django] static --> resp[Phase 5 Build Response] dynamic --> appExec[App Executes Business Logic] appExec --> resp resp --> log[Phase 6 Log Access] log --> send[Phase 7 Send Response to Client] ``` --- ## 4.5 SOAP vs REST — Side-by-Side Conceptual Map ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SOAP ["SOAP STACK"] s1[XML Message] --> s2[SOAP Envelope] s2 --> s3[WSDL Contract] s3 --> s4[UDDI Registry] s4 --> s5[HTTP SMTP Transport] end subgraph REST ["REST STACK"] r1[JSON or XML] --> r2[HTTP Methods] r2 --> r3[URI as Resource ID] r3 --> r4[OpenAPI Optional] r4 --> r5[HTTP HTTPS Only] end ``` --- ## 4.6 Hosting Options — Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD start[New Web Project] --> q1{Need Full OS
Control?} q1 -- Yes --> q2{Traffic Pattern?} q1 -- No --> q3{Steady
Workload?} q2 -- Spiky --> serverless[Serverless
AWS Lambda Azure Functions] q2 -- Steady --> iaas[IaaS
AWS EC2 Azure VM] q3 -- Yes --> paas[PaaS
Heroku App Engine Beanstalk] q3 -- No --> serverless serverless --> end1[Cost Lowest
Cold Start Risk] iaas --> end2[Most Control
Highest Maintenance] paas --> end3[Balanced
Pay Per Use] ``` --- ## 4.7 SOAP Fault Handling Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB req[SOAP Request Received] --> process[Server Processes Request] process --> check{Error
Occurred?} check -- No --> ok[Build Normal Response Envelope] check -- Yes --> fault[Build Fault Element] fault --> fcode[Set Fault Code
Client Server VersionMismatch] fault --> fstring[Set Fault String
Human Readable] fault --> fdetail[Set Detail Element
App Specific Error] ok --> send[Send HTTP 200] fcode --> send500[Send HTTP 500] fstring --> send500 fdetail --> send500 send --> done[Response Delivered] send500 --> done ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Explain the term "Web Service" with two examples.** **Model Answer:** A web service is a software system designed to enable interoperable machine-to-machine communication over a network using open, standard protocols (HTTP, XML, JSON, SOAP, WSDL). It is platform-independent and loosely coupled, allowing applications written in different languages to interact seamlessly. **Two Examples:** 1. **Google Maps API** — A RESTful web service that lets applications embed maps, geocode addresses, and compute routes. 2. **PayPal Payment Gateway** — A SOAP/REST web service that processes online payments across merchant systems. > [!NOTE] > **Mark Distribution (KTU pattern):** Definition (1 mark) + Characteristics (1 mark) + Examples (1 mark). --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **Differentiate between SOAP and REST (any three points).** **Model Answer:** | Sl. | SOAP | REST | |---|---|---| | 1 | SOAP is a **protocol** with strict standards (WSDL, UDDI). | REST is an **architectural style** defined by constraints. | | 2 | Supports only **XML** as the message format. | Supports **JSON, XML, YAML, plain text**. | | 3 | Slower and uses more **bandwidth** due to verbose XML envelopes. | Faster and **lightweight**, ideal for mobile and web. | | 4 | Built-in error handling via the **``** element. | Error handling via **HTTP status codes** (404, 500, etc.). | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Warning:** Writing "REST is a protocol" will cost you the full 3 marks. Always emphasize that REST is an *architectural style*, not a protocol. --- ## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has an **internal choice** — answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each carries sub-parts (a) and (b) worth 7 marks each. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a SOAP-based web service. List the layers involved. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write a complete SOAP request and response XML message for a "Celsius to Fahrenheit" conversion web service. Show the fault case for invalid input. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) The SOAP architecture consists of four major layers: 1. **Service Transport Layer:** Defines the protocol (HTTP, SMTP, TCP, JMS) used to carry SOAP messages. 2. **XML Messaging Layer:** Encodes messages in XML, wrapped inside a `` containing an optional `` and a mandatory ``. 3. **Service Description Layer (WSDL):** The Web Services Description Language is an XML-based interface definition language that describes the operations, parameters, and data types of a web service. 4. **Service Discovery Layer (UDDI):** Universal Description, Discovery and Integration is a registry where businesses can publish and find web services. **Diagram:** ```mermaid flowchart TB A[CLIENT APPLICATION] --> B[UDDI REGISTRY
Find Service] B --> C[WSDL DOCUMENT
Understand Contract] C --> D[SOAP REQUEST
XML over HTTP] D --> E[WEB SERVER
Apache NGINX] E --> F[APP SERVER
JAX-WS CXF] F --> G[(DATABASE)] F --> H[SOAP RESPONSE
XML Envelope] H --> A ``` **Layer Roles Summary Table:** | Layer | Function | Example | |---|---|---| | Transport | Carries SOAP envelopes | HTTP, SMTP | | XML Messaging | Encodes the message | SOAP Envelope | | Description | Describes interface | WSDL | | Discovery | Locates services | UDDI | #### Model Solution — Part (b) **SOAP Request (Celsius to Fahrenheit, Input = 100°C):** ```xml 100 ``` **SOAP Successful Response:** $$ F = \frac{9}{5} \times 100 + 32 = 212\,^{\circ}\text{F} $$ ```xml 212 ``` **SOAP Fault Response (Input = "abc"):** ```xml soap:Client Invalid input: not a number INVALID_INPUT Expected a numeric value for Celsius. ``` > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget the mandatory `` element. Without it, the message is not a valid SOAP envelope. Also remember the **namespace declaration** `xmlns:soap="..."` — omitting it loses a mark. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **(a)** Explain the REST architectural style. List and briefly describe any four of its constraints. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a suitable example, demonstrate how a RESTful web service exposes resources. Show the HTTP request and JSON response for retrieving and updating a "Book" resource. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **REST (Representational State Transfer)** is an architectural style introduced by **Roy Fielding** in his 2000 PhD dissertation. It defines a set of constraints that, when applied, produce a distributed system with desirable properties like scalability, modifiability, and visibility. **Four Key REST Constraints:** 1. **Client-Server:** Separates the user interface (client) from data storage (server). This separation allows them to evolve independently — e.g., a mobile app and a web app can both consume the same REST API. 2. **Stateless:** Each request from client to server must contain all information necessary to understand the request. The server does not store any client session state between requests. This simplifies server design and improves scalability. 3. **Cacheable:** Responses must indicate whether they are cacheable. A well-managed cache eliminates partial or entire client-server interactions, improving efficiency and perceived performance. 4. **Uniform Interface:** A standardized way of communicating between client and server using: - Resource identification (URIs) - Resource representation (JSON/XML) - Standard HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) - Hypermedia as the engine of application state (HATEOAS) #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Scenario:** A `Book` resource in an online library REST API. **Resource URI:** ``` https://api.library.example.com/books/101 ``` **HTTP GET — Retrieve Book 101:** ```http GET /books/101 HTTP/1.1 Host: api.library.example.com Accept: application/json ``` **JSON Response:** ```json { "id": 101, "title": "Clean Code", "author": "Robert C. Martin", "isbn": "978-0132350884", "available": true } ``` **HTTP PUT — Update Book 101:** ```http PUT /books/101 HTTP/1.1 Host: api.library.example.com Content-Type: application/json { "title": "Clean Code", "author": "Robert C. Martin", "isbn": "978-0132350884", "available": false } ``` **Response after update:** ```json { "id": 101, "title": "Clean Code", "author": "Robert C. Martin", "isbn": "978-0132350884", "available": false, "updatedAt": "2024-11-20T10:30:00Z" } ``` > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse `PUT` and `PATCH`. **PUT replaces the entire resource**, while **PATCH applies a partial update**. Misusing them loses you marks in API design questions. Also remember: **GET must be safe and idempotent** — never modify state inside a GET handler. --- ## ✅ KTU Common Pitfalls — Quick-Reference Warning > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 Ways KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic:** > 1. Calling **REST a "protocol"** instead of an "architectural style" — instant -2 marks. > 2. Forgetting the **namespace declaration** in SOAP envelopes. > 3. Using `GET` to delete/update a resource (violates REST's safe and idempotent principles). > 4. Confusing **WSDL with UDDI** (WSDL = description; UDDI = discovery). > 5. Saying "web server and app server are the same" — they are NOT. The web server handles HTTP routing and static content; the app server executes business logic. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Final Revision Checklist — Module 4: Web Services** ### 🔹 Overview of Web Services - A web service is a **standardized, interoperable, language-independent** system for machine-to-machine communication. - Built on **open protocols** (HTTP, XML, JSON, SOAP, WSDL). - **Loosely coupled, self-describing, discoverable, composable**. ### 🔹 SOAP Services - **SOAP = Simple Object Access Protocol** — a protocol, NOT an architecture. - Uses **XML** exclusively for messages. - Built on a **4-layer stack**: Transport, XML Messaging, WSDL, UDDI. - Mandatory message structure: `` → `` (with optional `` and ``). - Strengths: **Built-in security (WS-Security), built-in error handling, supports multiple transports**. - Weaknesses: **Verbose, slower, heavier**. ### 🔹 REST Services - **REST = Representational State Transfer** — an *architectural style* by Roy Fielding (2000). - 6 Constraints: **Client-Server, Stateless, Cacheable, Uniform Interface, Layered System, Code-on-Demand**. - Uses **HTTP verbs**: GET (read), POST (create), PUT (replace), PATCH (partial update), DELETE (remove). - Supports **JSON, XML, YAML** as data formats. - Strengths: **Lightweight, fast, scalable, ideal for mobile and public APIs**. - Weaknesses: **No built-in contract (WSDL), no built-in security, no built-in error semantics**. ### 🔹 SOAP vs REST — One-Line Memory Aid > *"SOAP is a strict, formal contract; REST is a flexible, conversational handshake."* ### 🔹 Example Web Service - **Temperature Conversion**: $F = \frac{9}{5}C + 32$ is the canonical KTU example. - Demonstrates **statelessness, idempotency, and pure function** behavior. - Easily implemented in both SOAP (XML) and REST (JSON) styles. ### 🔹 Web Server - Software that **accepts HTTP requests and returns responses**. - **Static content** served directly; **dynamic content** forwarded to app servers. - Popular choices: **Apache (process-based), NGINX (event-based), IIS, Tomcat**. ### 🔹 Hosting Options - **Shared Hosting** — Cheapest, but no control. Best for: small static sites. - **VPS** — Dedicated virtual slice. Best for: medium-traffic apps. - **Dedicated Server** — Entire physical machine. Best for: high-traffic, compliance-heavy apps. - **Cloud PaaS** — Managed platform. Best for: modern web apps (Heroku, Elastic Beanstalk). - **Serverless** — Function-as-a-Service. Best for: spiky, event-driven workloads (AWS Lambda). - **Containerized** — Docker + Kubernetes. Best for: microservices architectures. ### 🔹 Key Formulas / Equations - Temperature conversion: $F = \frac{9}{5}C + 32$ - Linear scale conversion general: $F = m \cdot C + b$ where $m$ is the scale ratio and $b$ is the offset. ### 🔹 KTU Viva Favorites 1. *"Why is REST called stateless?"* — Because each request contains all info; the server holds no client state. 2. *"What is the role of WSDL?"* — It is the *contract* of a SOAP service, describing operations and data types. 3. *"Why has UDDI declined in popularity?"* — Replaced by API Gateways (Kong, Apigee) and Service Registries (Consul, Eureka) in modern microservice architectures. 4. *"Apache vs NGINX — which is faster under high concurrency?"* — NGINX, due to its event-driven architecture. --- **End of Module 4 Notes — Web Services** 🎓
